quotes.quote: I often think 'why can't we be as easy to use as Heroku?' and then try to make us a little more like that.
quotes.summary: Evans explores what she learned by examining Skyliner, a platform that deploys code to AWS instances, treating it as a case study in good architecture decisions. She discovers that new AWS instances start up faster than expected (under 5 minutes), that Docker can be used simply by avoiding its complex features like container networking and orchestration, and that AWS Application Load Balancers have useful APIs for managing deploys. The post advocates for studying other people's architecture choices as a way to learn, even if you don't use their product directly.
quotes.key_insight: Studying how others have assembled complex platforms like AWS into coherent products is a powerful way to learn which features actually matter and which complexity you can safely avoid.

quotes.key_insight: Studying how others have assembled complex platforms like AWS into coherent products is a powerful way to learn which features actually matter and which complexity you can safely avoid.

quotes.quote: I've found they're, in some situations, such a great way to communicate that I feel silly not taking advantage of it
quotes.summary: Evans responds to criticism of her simple technical drawings by explaining why they're effective despite not being artistically sophisticated. She argues that comics excel at summarizing complex topics, lowering barriers to learning, and introducing people to concepts they might otherwise overlook. She cites concrete examples: a comic that condensed a 15-minute blog post into 30 seconds of understanding, and a /proc drawing that reached 180,000 people on Twitter. She originally started drawing due to wrist pain but continues because the format demonstrably works for teaching.
quotes.key_insight: The value of simple technical drawings isn't artistic quality but their ability to summarize, lower barriers, and introduce people to concepts they'd otherwise never encounter.

quotes.quote: I've claimed previously on this blog that rkt 'just runs programs, it's not that complicated, that's why it's cool'. But if you look at the source for rkt, excluding tests, there are about 34,000 lines of Go code today.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans traces the complete lifecycle of running a rkt container, from fetching and storing images to preparing pods and finally executing the containerized process. She examines how rkt maintains a local image store backed by a SQL database, then pre-renders container filesystems into a 'tree store' (doubling disk usage) due to ACI's DAG-based dependency format. The post walks through overlay filesystem mounting for pods and shows the actual system calls that culminate in a chroot and execve. She deliberately chose the simplest possible options (disabling security, using the lightweight 'fly' stage1) to make the internals tractable, and argues that understanding your infrastructure at this level is valuable when you're the person responsible for operating it.
quotes.key_insight: Understanding the full lifecycle of your container runtime — from image storage to system calls — transforms capacity planning from guesswork into informed engineering.

quotes.quote: If you think this takes extra disk space, you are right! When you use rkt to run a container you get 2 copies of every file in that container.

quotes.quote: You can run containers without using Docker or Kubernetes or Mesos or anything like that.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans proposes a pragmatic migration plan for adopting containers: start by running containers without Docker, Kubernetes, or any orchestration layer. She argues for horizontal, incremental changes over building a 'Cool New World' cluster, suggesting teams use minimal tools like rkt to run container images in their existing infrastructure. The key insight is that containerizing applications is work you'd have to do anyway, so it can be separated into a smaller, less risky project. She outlines concrete benefits like reducing fragile Puppet configuration and eliminating hidden host dependencies, while acknowledging containers are worth learning about because that's where the ecosystem is heading.
quotes.key_insight: Containerizing your applications is valuable work on its own that you'd have to do anyway, so you can separate it from the much harder problem of adopting orchestration platforms.

quotes.quote: I do not contribute to open source in my spare time, I write this blog instead.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores a series of practical questions about open source software from the perspective of a tech company employee. She examines when to use open source vs building your own tools, why companies pay to develop open source, how to evaluate unknown software, and whether promoting software that doesn't work well is harmful. Rather than providing definitive answers, she lays out the trade-offs and complexities honestly, acknowledging that open source is both critically important and surprisingly confusing in practice.
quotes.key_insight: Open source software is critically important to the tech industry, but the practical questions of when to use it, how to evaluate it, and understanding the incentives behind it are surprisingly complex and under-discussed.

quotes.quote: We changed our mind about what we wanted out of the system, and that helped us operate it better!
quotes.summary: Evans explains the tension between consistency and availability in distributed systems through a personal story about a database that kept going down at work. Rather than diving into the formal CAP theorem, she builds intuition: consistent systems require machines to communicate and agree, which is hard when networks are unreliable, while available systems can simply serve data from any machine without coordination. The key revelation is that her team's system stopped going down once they dropped consistency as a requirement, accepting slightly stale data in exchange for reliability. She argues that good distributed systems engineering means being extremely careful about a small number of consistent systems and making everything else as simple as possible.
quotes.key_insight: Many systems don't actually need consistency, and recognizing that lets you trade it for availability — solving operational problems not through better engineering but through better requirements.

quotes.summary: Evans explains the tension between consistency and availability in distributed systems through a personal story about a database that kept going down at work. Rather than diving into the formal CAP theorem, she builds intuition: consistent systems require machines to communicate and agree, which is hard when networks are unreliable, while available systems can simply serve data from any machine without coordination. The key revelation is that her team's system stopped going down once they dropped consistency as a requirement, accepting slightly stale data in exchange for reliability. She argues that good distributed systems engineering means being extremely careful about a small number of consistent systems and making everything else as simple as possible.
quotes.key_insight: Many systems don't actually need consistency, and recognizing that lets you trade it for availability — solving operational problems not through better engineering but through better requirements.

quotes.quote: Would everyone on your team today have met those requirements when they joined?
quotes.summary: Evans proposes a simple test for job descriptions: would everyone currently on the team have met the listed requirements when they joined? She uses her own experience switching to infrastructure work with zero prior experience in her team's specific tools as evidence that many listed requirements aren't actual requirements. She argues that while overly broad 'anyone can learn anything' claims aren't helpful either, teams should be honest about what they actually need rather than listing aspirational or inflated requirements.
quotes.key_insight: Job descriptions should list only requirements that every current team member actually met when they were hired, forcing honesty about what's truly needed versus what's aspirational.

quotes.quote: Puppet is software, communication is about how humans work together.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores what 'devops' actually means after reading 'Effective DevOps' by Jennifer Davis and Ryn Daniels. She clarifies that devops is fundamentally about communication and collaboration between development and operations teams, not about specific tools like Chef or Puppet. The post distinguishes devops from job titles, automation tooling, and the idea that operations should be made so easy no expertise is needed. Evans reflects on how many devops practices were invisible to her because her organization already followed them, making her realize these were deliberate choices rather than defaults. She concludes that devops is an evolving set of practices the industry is learning together.
quotes.key_insight: DevOps is fundamentally about how people with different expertise collaborate effectively, not about any particular set of tools, and recognizing your organizational practices as deliberate choices opens the door to continuous improvement.

quotes.quote: I definitely went from 'operations is trivial, whatever, how hard can keeping a site up be?' to 'OMG THIS IS IMPOSSIBLE HOW DOES ANYONE EVER DO THIS'.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans walks through her personal journey of understanding operations as a software developer, from not knowing what it was to recognizing it as a learnable skill. She describes three stages: thinking software just works, being terrified that anything can break, and realizing there's a body of knowledge for keeping systems running. She argues that learning to be appropriately scared of dangerous changes is important, but you can't stop there—you need to learn the precautionary steps to make changes confidently. The post advocates for developers taking on operations work as a way to become better programmers and debuggers.
quotes.key_insight: Operations is a learnable skillset that progresses from ignorance through healthy fear to competence, and developers who embrace it become significantly better engineers.

quotes.quote: The word "container" doesn't mean anything super precise. Basically there are a few new Linux kernel features ("namespaces" and "cgroups") that let you isolate processes from each other. When you use those features, you call it "containers".
quotes.summary: Julia Evans demystifies containers by breaking them down into their underlying Linux kernel primitives: namespaces, cgroups, and seccomp-bpf. She demonstrates that containers aren't virtual machines but rather isolated processes using these kernel features. Through hands-on examples, she shows how to create a PID namespace with unshare, limit memory with cgroups, and explains how seccomp-bpf restricts system calls. The post concludes that Docker is essentially tooling built on top of these basic primitives.
quotes.key_insight: Containers are not virtual machines or magic — they're just ordinary Linux processes isolated using namespaces, cgroups, and seccomp-bpf, and you can create them yourself with simple command-line tools.

quotes.quote: Basically these features let you pretend you have something like a virtual machine, except it's not a virtual machine at all, it's just processes running in the same Linux kernel.

quotes.quote: problem: Jekyll generated my site slowly. It took, like, 10 seconds! Who has time to wait for that!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her migration from Jekyll to Hugo for her blog's static site generation. The switch was motivated by Jekyll's slow 10-second build times and frustrating Ruby dependency management. She completed the migration in about 5 hours using a pragmatic approach: rather than understanding her Jekyll theme, she copied the rendered HTML output and worked backwards. The result was a 0.4-second build time and a simpler setup she actually understands. A 2023 update confirms she's still happily using Hugo seven years later, still on version 0.40 from 2018.
quotes.key_insight: The best migration strategy is often working backwards from what you have rather than trying to understand and port the system you're replacing.

quotes.quote: Instead of having children, what I do is you have a child that is a clone of myself, and then that child gets its brain eaten and turns into ls.
quotes.summary: The post explains how Linux starts new processes using the fork and exec system calls rather than a single 'start process' call. Fork creates a clone of the current process, then exec replaces that clone's memory and program with the new one. Evans uses the memorable metaphor of a child clone getting its 'brain eaten' to explain how exec works. She covers the zombie process state, copy-on-write optimization for fork, and which process attributes survive across exec. The post concludes by showing why understanding this mechanism matters for debugging real bugs, like inherited signal handlers causing unexpected behavior.
quotes.key_insight: Linux starts processes not by creating them from scratch but by cloning the parent with fork and then replacing the clone's program with exec, which means new processes inherit most of their parent's attributes — and understanding this inheritance is key to debugging subtle bugs.

quotes.quote: At this point ls is actually a zombie process! That means it's dead, but it's waiting around for me in case I want to check on its return value.

quotes.key_insight: Linux starts processes not by creating them from scratch but by cloning the parent with fork and then replacing the clone's program with exec, which means new processes inherit most of their parent's attributes — and understanding this inheritance is key to debugging subtle bugs.

quotes.quote: Your program's environment (environment, signal handlers, etc.) can matter! It inherits its environment from its parent process, whatever that was!
quotes.summary: The post explains how Linux starts new processes using the fork and exec system calls rather than a single 'start process' call. Fork creates a clone of the current process, then exec replaces that clone's memory and program with the new one. Evans uses the memorable metaphor of a child clone getting its 'brain eaten' to explain how exec works. She covers the zombie process state, copy-on-write optimization for fork, and which process attributes survive across exec. The post concludes by showing why understanding this mechanism matters for debugging real bugs, like inherited signal handlers causing unexpected behavior.
quotes.key_insight: Linux starts processes not by creating them from scratch but by cloning the parent with fork and then replacing the clone's program with exec, which means new processes inherit most of their parent's attributes — and understanding this inheritance is key to debugging subtle bugs.

quotes.quote: The concerns when you run containers in production are pretty different from running it on a laptop.
quotes.summary: Evans walks through the hidden complexity behind 'just running a container' in production, starting from the simplest case (runC) and progressively discovering requirements like overlay filesystems, image verification, and process supervision. She compares Docker and rkt architectures, explaining why Docker's monolithic approach is being broken apart into components like containerd. The post traces her learning journey through each layer of the container stack, showing that what sounds simple actually involves kernel version requirements, filesystem drivers, security signing, and process management. She concludes that running containers without Docker is possible but far from simple, and understanding the components helps demystify the ecosystem.
quotes.key_insight: What appears to be a simple request—'just run a container'—actually involves overlay filesystems, kernel requirements, image verification, and process supervision, and understanding each layer demystifies the entire container ecosystem.

quotes.quote: I have been confused about the plethora of container software in the world.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans catalogs the container software ecosystem to make sense of the overwhelming number of tools available. She lists 17 pieces of software from major organizations including Docker, CoreOS, HashiCorp, Google, Mesosphere, and OCI. By writing everything down and summarizing each tool in three words or less, she finds the landscape less intimidating than it initially seemed. The post serves as a reference for anyone confused by the proliferation of container-related projects.
quotes.key_insight: The container ecosystem feels less overwhelming when you enumerate every tool explicitly — the actual list is smaller and more organized than the vague sense of chaos suggests.

quotes.quote: He suggested just picking a vision that seemed mostly reasonable and then moving that way until I learned more. This was actually really helpful.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares personal productivity strategies that help her get things done as an individual software engineer on a small team. She emphasizes having a clear vision for your work, focusing on one or two things at a time, and breaking large goals into smaller steps. The post covers practical tactics like making daily progress on important work, asking for help when stuck, not being afraid of unexpected problems, and being willing to do unfamiliar work across team boundaries. She frames deadlines as useful scope-management tools rather than threats, and closes by noting that believing in your own ability to deliver is both reasonable and productive.
quotes.key_insight: Productivity as an individual engineer comes from having a clear (even imperfect) vision, ruthlessly focusing on one thing at a time, and staying optimistic through the inevitable maze of unexpected problems.

quotes.quote: When I actually focus on a thing, I'm always amazed by how much I can get done. If I do more than about 2 things, then all of the things take forever to get finished.

quotes.quote: I used to think to debug things, I had to be really really smart. I thought I had to stare at the code, and think really hard, and magically intuit what the bug was.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans presents a talk from Strange Loop about using systems debugging tools to investigate programs without reading source code or even knowing what language they're written in. She demonstrates strace for tracing system calls, time and /proc for diagnosing slow programs, perf top for identifying CPU-intensive functions, dstat for monitoring disk and network activity, and ngrep for inspecting network traffic. Through a series of mysteries (wrong config files, slow programs, French websites), she shows how these tools let you ask questions of the operating system to get concrete answers. Her core message is that debugging doesn't require genius-level intuition — it requires the right tools and a willingness to not be intimidated by systems-level concepts.
quotes.key_insight: You don't need to be a genius to debug complex problems — you just need the right systems tools to ask your operating system what's actually happening.

quotes.quote: Installing stuff on computers so you can run your program on them really sucks. It's easy to get wrong! It's scary when you make changes!
quotes.summary: Evans explores the landscape of running containers in production, comparing Docker and rkt while candidly admitting she has many unanswered questions. She identifies packaging as the most compelling reason to use containers, since installing dependencies on servers is error-prone and painful. She raises concerns about Docker's daemon architecture, where bugs can deadlock and kill all containers, versus rkt's simpler process model. The post also surfaces subtle gotchas like PID 1 behavior, networking complexity, and secrets management. Her overall assessment from talking to practitioners is that containers are genuinely useful despite the hype, but full of sharp edges.
quotes.key_insight: Containers solve the real pain of packaging and deployment, but their abstractions violate familiar Unix assumptions in ways that create surprising sharp edges in production.

quotes.quote: I have 2 CS degrees but didn't know what a system call was when I graduated.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explains how she earned two CS degrees without ever learning what a system call is, not as a failure of education but as a reflection of the breadth possible within CS programs. She lists all her undergrad and grad courses, showing she focused heavily on pure math, algorithms, and CS theory rather than systems programming. Her main point is that 'a CS degree' can represent very different educations depending on what you choose to study, and that's perfectly fine. She emphasizes that it's okay to not know things, and that people learn what they need when they need it.
quotes.key_insight: A CS degree represents just one particular path through a vast field, and the gaps it leaves are not failures but opportunities for continued learning.

quotes.key_insight: A CS degree represents just one particular path through a vast field, and the gaps it leaves are not failures but opportunities for continued learning.

quotes.quote: I have friends who are amazing programmers who sometimes feel bad because they don't have a CS degree and sometimes don't know algorithms/CS theory stuff that other people know. They've learned the things they needed to know! They are great.

Her main point is that 'a CS degree' can represent very different educations depending on what you choose to study, and that's perfectly fine. quotes.key_insight: A CS degree represents just one particular path through a vast field, and the gaps it leaves are not failures but opportunities for continued learning.

quotes.quote: I spend a lot of time being frustrated with the Rust compiler, but I still like it because it lets me do things I probably wouldn't get done otherwise.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans delivered the closing keynote at the first RustConf about how Rust empowers people who don't consider themselves systems programmers to learn and experiment with systems concepts. She shares her journey from not knowing what a system call was to writing an operating system keyboard driver and a Ruby profiling tool in Rust. She argues that Rust's memory safety lets programmers tackle projects that would be impractical in C, and that you don't need to be an expert to teach others — beginners writing about what they learn can be enormously valuable. The talk emphasizes that systems programming concepts aren't as hard as people think, and that the Rust community is uniquely positioned to help experienced developers level up.
quotes.key_insight: Rust empowers people who aren't traditional systems programmers to experiment with and learn systems concepts that would be impractical to tackle in C, and sharing what you learn as a non-expert is one of the most valuable contributions you can make.

quotes.quote: This zine is about some of my favorite Linux debugging tools, especially tools that I don't think are as well-known as they should be.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans announces a new zine about Linux debugging tools that she thinks deserve more attention. The zine covers strace, opensnoop/eBPF, dstat, netcat, netstat, tcpdump, wireshark, ngrep, and perf. It's designed to be accessible to both beginners and experienced Linux users, and she's been pleased to see people at all experience levels learning from it. The zine is available in multiple formats including printable versions.
quotes.key_insight: Powerful Linux debugging tools like strace, perf, and tcpdump are underappreciated, and making them accessible through approachable formats can help developers at every experience level.

quotes.quote: One thing about asking questions that I think doesn't always get enough appreciation is that asking good questions is a skill. It's something that you can practice and get better at.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans argues that asking good questions is a learnable skill that takes practice and deliberate effort. She shares strategies for formulating better questions, including summarizing your current understanding, asking about history and edge cases, and providing partial answers to prompt deeper responses. She emphasizes that being humble about knowledge gaps is essential, and that good questions serve not just the asker but everyone around them. The post catalogs specific question templates that reliably surface interesting knowledge.
quotes.key_insight: Asking good questions is a practicable skill, and specific question templates — like summarizing your understanding, asking why something is hard, or providing partial answers — reliably surface deeper knowledge than open-ended questions.

quotes.quote: Sometimes if there's a topic I'm really confused about, I need to concentrate for like 10 minutes to actually come up with a more coherent question that "what? I don't get it?".

quotes.quote: Fun, accessible content works. People understand it.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans describes the process of creating her programming zines, from initial inspiration through tools, printing, and distribution. She traces her motivation to feminist publishing culture and riot grrrl zines, combined with a desire to make intimidating technical topics like strace and tcpdump accessible. She details her evolution from hand-drawn sharpie-on-paper to a Samsung tablet with stylus, and explains the PDF toolchain for turning images into printable booklets. The post argues that fun, approachable formats lower barriers to learning topics traditionally considered advanced.
quotes.key_insight: Making technical content fun and visually approachable removes the intimidation factor that keeps people from learning tools they could have learned years earlier.

quotes.quote: When you're losing UDP packets, it's sort of tempting to say 'well, whatever, that's what happens when you use UDP!' But UDP packets don't get lost by magic.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explains the three places where UDP packets can be dropped: on the way out (socket send buffer overflow), in transit across the internet, and on the way in (socket receive buffer overflow). She walks through Linux sysctls that control buffer sizes and shows how to use netstat to monitor dropped packets. The post demystifies packet loss by emphasizing that packets don't disappear by magic — they get dropped because buffers get full. She concludes by noting that everything in networking is buffers, and TCP packets get dropped just as easily but are retried automatically.
quotes.key_insight: UDP packet loss isn't mysterious — packets get dropped at specific, observable points where buffers overflow, and Linux gives you tools to monitor exactly where and how often it happens.

quotes.quote: Instead of writing down a list of goals, instead I wrote a release email
quotes.summary: Evans describes a technique where instead of writing abstract project goals in a design document, she writes a mock release email announcing the finished feature. She found this approach more fun and motivating than traditional goal-setting, and it forced her to think about narrative, audience, and value proposition. The technique also generated immediate helpful feedback from colleagues. She acknowledges this is similar to 'documentation driven development' but finds personal value in the specific framing.
quotes.key_insight: Writing a mock release announcement before building a feature is a motivating alternative to abstract goal-setting that forces you to articulate narrative, audience, and value.

The technique also generated immediate helpful feedback from colleagues. She acknowledges this is similar to 'documentation driven development' but finds personal value in the specific framing.
quotes.key_insight: Writing a mock release announcement before building a feature is a motivating alternative to abstract goal-setting that forces you to articulate narrative, audience, and value.

quotes.quote: The reason programmers sometimes get paid a lot of money, I think, is because we can build systems that leverage computers to do an unreasonable amount of work.
quotes.summary: Evans reflects on the challenge of deciding what to work on as a software engineer, arguing that choosing the right projects matters enormously because meaningful work takes months and you can only do a few things per year. She advocates for having a clear, explainable focus area while acknowledging there's no single 'right thing' to work on. The post emphasizes that believing a project is possible is essential for finishing ambitious work, and that the transition from school to employment means you must grade your own tests. She candidly admits she still struggles with this and that many of her best contributions came from getting excited about other people's ideas.
quotes.key_insight: Choosing what to work on is one of the hardest and most consequential skills in engineering — harder than the actual building — and it requires developing a clear focus, believing ambitious projects are possible, and learning to grade your own work.

quotes.quote: The more time I spend programming, the more time I see that it's actually super hard to figure out what would be important to work on.

quotes.quote: If we want to find the address of a global variable in our program, all we need to do is look up the name of the variable in the symbol table, and then add that to the start of the range in /proc/whatever/maps, and we're done!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explains the internal mechanisms gdb uses to debug programs, walking through concrete examples with a Ruby process. She shows how gdb finds global variables by combining symbol table offsets with memory-mapped addresses from /proc/pid/maps, how it reads memory using the ptrace system call, and how it interprets raw bytes as meaningful struct fields using DWARF debugging data. She also touches on gdb's stack unwinding capabilities and notes that understanding these internals gives her a better mental model for using gdb effectively.
quotes.key_insight: gdb works by combining symbol tables, ptrace system calls, and DWARF debugging data to translate raw process memory into the meaningful variable names and struct fields that developers see.

quotes.quote: I love learning about programming problems (that are solved on my computer every day, when serializing numbers into JSON!) that I didn't even know existed.
quotes.summary: Evans discusses the challenges of correct benchmarking, inspired by a paper on rigorous benchmarking and a floating-point printing paper that had incorrect performance claims. She highlights that many performance improvements are small (5-10%), which falls within typical measurement error, making confidence intervals essential. The post explores the concept of independence in benchmark samples, explaining that sequential runs may not be independent due to JIT warmup, periodic patterns, or other effects. She presents a visual smoke test for checking independence: randomly shuffling benchmark results and comparing them to the original sequence. The core message is that understanding your benchmark data matters more than blindly computing averages.
quotes.key_insight: Benchmarking correctly requires understanding statistical properties of your data—checking for independence, using confidence intervals, and visualizing results over time—rather than blindly averaging numbers.

quotes.quote: The difference between 17-year old Julia and 25-year-old Julia is that 25-year-old Julia knew how HTTP works.
quotes.summary: Evans explains how web servers route HTTP requests to the right site using the Host header, then builds up to the more complex problem of TLS/SNI. She starts by showing how to make raw HTTP requests with netcat, demonstrating that the Host header is the simple mechanism behind virtual hosting. She then explains why HTTPS breaks this model—the Host header is encrypted, so the server can't read it before choosing a certificate—and how SNI solves this by including the hostname in the unencrypted TLS Client Hello packet. She verifies her understanding by reading nginx source code, discovering that virtual server lookup happens twice per TLS request.
quotes.key_insight: Understanding what an HTTP request actually looks like makes web server configuration intuitive rather than magical, and SNI extends that same routing concept into the encrypted TLS handshake.

quotes.quote: If you remove the Host: part from this request, it does not work. Zero. Apache is all '400 Bad request'. 400 means it is your fault.

quotes.quote: Talking about it in public means that other people can learn from your experience instead of always having to start from scratch.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recaps her experience at PolyConf 2016, a multilingual programming conference in Poland. She highlights talks on Elm, Erlang, Datomic, high-performance HTTP servers, and Guile Scheme, while appreciating both the enthusiastic and critical perspectives presented. She shares her own talk on systems programming and proposes 'rules of programming experiments' that encourage learning through unfinished, imperfect projects. The post celebrates the value of conferences for meeting people and encountering unfamiliar ideas across programming language boundaries.
quotes.key_insight: The best conference talks balance enthusiasm for new technology with honest, critical accounts of what doesn't work, because sharing failures publicly saves everyone from learning the hard way.

quotes.quote: I think it's incredibly important to talk critically about databases since it's so hard to change your database, we all have a limited amount of time to experiment, and database integrity is so important.

quotes.quote: I think of these tools like a swiss army knife – if something on your system is wrong or slow, in any programming language, knowing how to use them can really help you out.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her five favorite Linux debugging tools: strace, dstat, tcpdump/wireshark, perf, and opensnoop. She frames them as a swiss army knife that works across any programming language when something on your system is wrong or slow. The post gives brief descriptions of each tool's purpose and links to deeper resources, including her own prior writing. She emphasizes practical utility over theoretical completeness, noting she's used nearly all of them to debug actual problems.
quotes.key_insight: A small set of system-level debugging tools — strace, dstat, tcpdump, perf, and opensnoop — forms a universal toolkit that works across any programming language to diagnose what's wrong or slow on a Linux system.

quotes.quote: strace is pretty broadly useful, but keep in mind it can really slow down your programs.

quotes.quote: Most of the time is spent talking to the Linux kernel and copying things back and forth.
quotes.summary: Evans explores why high-performance networking projects bypass the Linux kernel's TCP stack in favor of userspace implementations. Inspired by a talk about the Lwan web server and Seastar framework, she investigates through Twitter crowd-sourcing how companies like Google (Maglev) and high-frequency trading firms use userspace networking for massive speed gains. The post covers DPDK's polling approach, the locking overhead that makes kernel TCP slow, and the surprising insight that performance isn't the only reason — evolving TCP standards can't wait for kernel updates. She catalogs numerous open-source userspace TCP stacks while noting none are truly general-purpose yet.
quotes.key_insight: The Linux kernel's TCP stack trades raw performance for generality, and bypassing it with userspace networking unlocks massive speed gains by eliminating locking, context switches, and memory copies — though no general-purpose alternative exists yet.

quotes.quote: The TCP standard is evolving, and if you have to always use your kernel's TCP stack, that means you can NEVER EVOLVE.

quotes.quote: ltrace traces library calls. This is cool because there are a lot of important things that happen that don't go through the kernel!
quotes.summary: Julia describes using ltrace for the first time to debug a memory leak in a Rust async library that was leaking 500 bytes per HTTP request. She explains that ltrace traces library calls like malloc and free, which don't go through the kernel. After valgrind proved unhelpful with its complex stack traces, ltrace revealed allocations that were never freed. By combining ltrace output with strategic print statements, they narrowed the leak to specific .boxed() calls in the Rust code that allocated memory on the heap.
quotes.key_insight: ltrace provides a simple, readable view of memory allocations that can make finding memory leaks straightforward when more sophisticated tools like valgrind produce overwhelming output.

quotes.quote: When you get sent SIGKILL nobody communicates with you, you just die immediately.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores whether Unix signals should be feared, after being told by experienced developers that they shouldn't be. She explains how signal handlers work at the kernel level, then catalogs the specific dangers: interrupted system calls returning EINTR, concurrency issues since signal handlers can't safely use locks or malloc, and the restrictions on what functions are safe to call. She surveys important programs that use signals successfully (init, terminals, the JVM, unicorn) and examines signalfd as an alternative approach. She concludes that signals are not trivial but are manageable with care, and advocates replacing vague fear with specific knowledge of what can go wrong.
quotes.key_insight: The right response to a scary technical concept like Unix signals isn't avoidance but understanding the specific things that can go wrong, turning vague fear into actionable knowledge.

quotes.quote: Surprise! now you have a concurrent program! you need to be very careful if you modify anything in your signal handler code because WHO KNOWS what state that thing was in before you changed it.

quotes.quote: One of the most frustrating things to me is – when I run a Ruby or Python program, I can't find out what that program is doing RIGHT NOW.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores how to build a 'jstack for Ruby' — a tool that can inspect a running Ruby program's stack trace without pre-instrumentation. She walks through using gdb to read Ruby's internal data structures and extract stack frames from memory, then explains why gdb's ptrace-based approach is too heavyweight. She discovers the process_vm_readv system call, which can read another process's memory with minimal impact, and builds a prototype Rust program that samples Ruby stack traces every 10 milliseconds to generate flame graphs.
quotes.key_insight: You can build lightweight debugging tools for any language runtime by combining DWARF debug info with process_vm_readv to read process memory without stopping the target program.

quotes.quote: It's important to know about file descriptors, but when I tell people about strace, I always feel like it's kind of annoying! Why can't strace just tell me which file it wrote to, instead of hiding it behind some number?
quotes.summary: Julia Evans discovers the new -y flag in strace after upgrading from Ubuntu 12.04 to 16.04. The flag resolves file descriptor numbers into human-readable filenames in strace output, making it much easier to understand what files a program is reading from and writing to. She demonstrates this by tracing cp and mv commands, showing how cp reads and writes 128KB chunks between files while mv is just a single rename system call. She also highlights the -yy flag which resolves network socket file descriptors into IP addresses and ports.
quotes.key_insight: The strace -y flag is a significant usability improvement that replaces opaque file descriptor numbers with human-readable filenames, making system call tracing far more accessible.

quotes.quote: After you go once or twice you don't really go to the talks any more – you won't learn anything. Just hang out in the hallway!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores how individual speakers can give better conference talks and how organizers can build better conferences. She argues that good talks must teach the audience something new in an understandable way, and offers concrete strategies: thinking deeply about your audience, keeping talks short, getting honest feedback, and talking about things you didn't build. She advocates for 'hard talks' that push audience knowledge while remaining accessible to multiple skill levels. For organizers, she suggests lightning talk tracks, pairing speakers for practice, writing desired talk ideas on websites, and actively recruiting speakers from other communities.
quotes.key_insight: The best conference talks teach the audience something genuinely new in an understandable way, and both speakers and organizers should actively optimize for learning rather than polish.

quotes.quote: If you accept truly difficult talks by speakers who can make the content accessible to enough people, then I think you can end up with a conference that people love going to every year.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores how individual speakers can give better conference talks and how organizers can build better conferences. She argues that good talks must teach the audience something new in an understandable way, and offers concrete strategies: thinking deeply about your audience, keeping talks short, getting honest feedback, and talking about things you didn't build. She advocates for 'hard talks' that push audience knowledge while remaining accessible to multiple skill levels. For organizers, she suggests lightning talk tracks, pairing speakers for practice, writing desired talk ideas on websites, and actively recruiting speakers from other communities.
quotes.key_insight: The best conference talks teach the audience something genuinely new in an understandable way, and both speakers and organizers should actively optimize for learning rather than polish.

quotes.quote: I have written extensive notes so that I can make sure to ask you all the most important questions I have and we can have a SUPER PRODUCTIVE MEETING.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans describes her evolving relationship with design documents, moving from grumpiness about writing them to finding them genuinely useful. She identifies common frustrations — nobody reads them, feedback can be unconstructive bikeshedding, and they can't predict everything. Her key shift was reframing design docs as personal thinking tools and conversation starters rather than formal artifacts requiring thorough review. She also values them as archaeological records that explain past decisions when revisited months later.
quotes.key_insight: Design documents are most valuable not as formal review artifacts but as personal thinking tools, conversation starters, and archaeological records of why decisions were made.

quotes.quote: This idea that you can predict all the flaws in advance and design the perfect system is actually a total lie.

quotes.quote: I sorta think of this blog as an extended fanzine for existing software.
quotes.summary: Evans presents a simple framework for tackling hard programming problems, breaking them into three approaches: repurposing existing powerful software, stealing ideas from academic papers and implementing them yourself, and coming up with genuinely new ideas (academic research). She argues that the first approach is the most accessible and fun, using examples like integer linear programming and dtrace. She also highlights the special value of research labs that both invent and ship usable software, combining the best of all approaches.
quotes.key_insight: Most hard programming problems don't require inventing something new — they require discovering that powerful existing software or well-documented ideas already solve your problem.

quotes.quote: I sit down and write something and don't stop until I've published it.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her approach to tech blogging, explaining her motivations evolved from job-hunting strategy to learning tool and community builder. She advocates for writing short posts quickly (1-3 hours), publishing frequently without excessive editing, and maintaining a low bar for what counts as 'interesting enough' to write about. She addresses the fear of admitting ignorance publicly, arguing it actually accelerates learning. Her core philosophy treats blog posts as small lottery tickets — produce many, keep them short, and let volume do the work.
quotes.key_insight: Prolific, honest blogging that embraces ignorance and keeps a low bar for interestingness compounds into career capital and genuine learning far more effectively than polished, infrequent writing.

quotes.quote: Producing things that you think are good is like buying a lottery ticket. So I guess I try to make good things and buy many small lottery tickets.

quotes.quote: I became more convinced at this conference that sometimes people do really cool things (like training a machine learning model to play space invaders), and those things are actually possible to reproduce for yourself if you read the paper and invest a bunch of time!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares notes and resources from her PyData Berlin keynote about tricking neural networks and the dangers of black box models. She covers adversarial examples that fool neural networks, fun art projects using neural nets, and strategies for making ML models more interpretable. She emphasizes that reproducing cool ML research results is achievable with investment of time. The talk also addressed unintended consequences of ML systems and technical debt in production ML.
quotes.key_insight: Impressive ML research results are more reproducible than people assume, but the field still lacks good solutions for making black box models interpretable and safe.

quotes.quote: machine learning models are only as good as the data you put into them
quotes.summary: Evans argues that despite machine learning being powerful and exciting, people should maintain healthy skepticism about its outputs. She identifies three key reasons for skepticism: models are limited by their training data and its biases, ML programs can contain ordinary software bugs that get mistaken for sophisticated behavior, and even well-built models frequently produce wrong results. She uses relatable examples like predicting nationality from names and Google Photos misclassifying images to illustrate these points. The post encourages non-specialists to question ML results rather than deferring to the technology's perceived authority.
quotes.key_insight: Machine learning outputs deserve the same skepticism as any other software output — they can reflect biased data, contain bugs, or simply be wrong, and non-experts questioning results are often right to do so.

quotes.quote: Someone described my approach to blogging as 'fanfiction' recently, a description that I kind of loved.
quotes.summary: Evans describes her delightful experience submitting a pull request to the Rust libc project, where two bots made the process smooth and welcoming. The highfive bot automatically greeted her and assigned a reviewer, while homu (bors) handled test coordination and merging after reviewer approval. She highlights how these bots reduce maintainer burden, especially for repositories with long or flaky test suites. The post is a brief appreciation of how automation can make open source contribution more pleasant for everyone involved.
quotes.key_insight: Simple bots that greet contributors and automate test-and-merge workflows can transform the open source contribution experience from tedious to delightful.

quotes.quote: I often use strace to see what system calls it runs. In my few experiments so far, the Erlang virtual machine runs a TON of system calls and I'm not sure exactly what it's doing.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans investigates the Erlang virtual machine by comparing the system calls of 'hello world' programs written in C, Python, Java, and Erlang. She discovers that Erlang generates roughly 15,000 system calls compared to Java's 2,700, Python's 1,550, and C's 38. The types of system calls differ dramatically too: while other languages mostly do file operations and memory allocation, Erlang's top calls are futex and sched_yield, reflecting its concurrent nature. She also notices mysterious behaviors like Erlang binding to a TCP socket just to print hello world. The post demonstrates how system call analysis can reveal fundamental differences in language runtime design.
quotes.key_insight: Examining a program's system calls can reveal the fundamental design philosophy of its runtime, even if you've never written a line of code in that language.

quotes.quote: I was surprised that the Erlang VM runs 6 times as many system calls as Java – I think of Java as already being pretty heavyweight.

quotes.quote: I was looking at this Erlang problem with Victor and David and they had OS X machines and I was like 'dude I can't debug anything on OS X'.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans investigates the Erlang virtual machine by comparing the system calls of 'hello world' programs written in C, Python, Java, and Erlang. She discovers that Erlang generates roughly 15,000 system calls compared to Java's 2,700, Python's 1,550, and C's 38. The types of system calls differ dramatically too: while other languages mostly do file operations and memory allocation, Erlang's top calls are futex and sched_yield, reflecting its concurrent nature. She also notices mysterious behaviors like Erlang binding to a TCP socket just to print hello world. The post demonstrates how system call analysis can reveal fundamental differences in language runtime design.
quotes.key_insight: Examining a program's system calls can reveal the fundamental design philosophy of its runtime, even if you've never written a line of code in that language.

quotes.quote: I think he's right that learning Rust is easier than learning C.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans describes her second attempt at learning Rust, finding it dramatically easier than her first try in 2013. She credits both the language's maturation and a new learning strategy of pair programming with an experienced Rust developer, letting him handle the parts she didn't understand. She argues Rust is easier to learn than C for systems programming, noting that common tasks like hashmaps and string formatting are surprisingly approachable. She highlights Rust's helpful error messages, good documentation, and compatibility with tools like valgrind and perf.
quotes.key_insight: You don't need to understand everything about a language to start being productive in it — selectively ignoring complexity and relying on experts for the hard parts is a valid and effective learning strategy.

quotes.quote: It turns out that no, you do not use RSA to encrypt messages in practice, which is what Cory told me and what surprised me so much.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares how a coworker's lunchtime explanation corrected her misconception that RSA is used to directly encrypt messages in SSL/TLS. She learned that RSA is too slow for bulk encryption, so in practice it's used only to exchange session keys for faster symmetric ciphers like AES. This distinction between key exchange and bulk encryption is the core of how SSL cipher suites work. Understanding this made conversations about insecure TLS ciphers suddenly click for her.
quotes.key_insight: RSA isn't used to encrypt your actual messages in SSL — it's only used to securely exchange a session key for a faster symmetric cipher, which is why the choice of that cipher matters so much for security.

quotes.quote: We do a lot of web development; almost all of our services talk to each other with network requests. So a tool that can spy on network requests can be a really good general-purpose debugging tool.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her notes from running a workshop teaching coworkers how to use tcpdump and strace for debugging. The tcpdump section progresses from basic packet capture on localhost to analyzing production traffic in Wireshark for performance debugging. The strace section covers reading system call output, finding configuration files, attaching to running processes, and diagnosing performance problems. She reflects that the hour-long format worked for experienced users but was too fast for beginners, and plans to iterate on the workshop format.
quotes.key_insight: tcpdump and strace are essential debugging tools that let you spy on network traffic and system calls respectively, turning opaque production problems into visible, diagnosable evidence.

quotes.quote: It's a public commitment to having good site performance.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans highlights Etsy's quarterly site performance reports published on their Code as Craft blog, praising them as a model of transparency and engineering rigor. She values their public commitment to performance, detailed graphs, explanations of what internal changes caused specific improvements, and honest discussion of measurement tool tradeoffs. She compiles an index of all historical performance reports dating back to 2011. She also notes that the Etsy performance team is mostly women, with Lara Hogan as Senior Engineering Manager, and links to related talks and resources on web performance culture.
quotes.key_insight: Publishing regular, transparent performance reports with detailed graphs and root cause analysis creates a powerful public accountability mechanism for engineering teams.

quotes.quote: There are a bajillion people who know amazing things, and often that knowledge gets stuck in their head and other people don't end up learning it. For years, sometimes!
quotes.summary: Evans explores why knowledge gets trapped in individuals' heads at work and how teams can spread expertise more broadly. She identifies discovery as a major problem — knowing who knows what is surprisingly difficult even among close colleagues. She examines why system builders become permanent experts and why others struggle to develop the same depth, pointing to historical context and a sense of entitlement to understanding. She proposes several practical solutions: directing questions to new people to build them into 'caches' of knowledge, apprenticeship models, internal talks, and the magical tactic of asking 'how did you find that out?' after someone solves a problem.
quotes.key_insight: Knowledge gets locked in people's heads not because they're unwilling to share, but because discovery of who knows what is broken, and organizations default to single experts instead of deliberately investing in building new ones through question-routing, apprenticeships, and extracting tacit knowledge.

quotes.quote: You are in New York, and the speed of light is slow (really!), and you are trying to send the javascript file to Sydney.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores the non-caching benefits of CDNs after joining her company's infrastructure team. She explains how CDNs speed up TLS handshakes by terminating connections at edge servers closer to users, use private network infrastructure for smarter routing, and provide DDoS mitigation. She also discusses the security tradeoffs of handing SSL certificates to a CDN, including government surveillance concerns. The post acknowledges downsides like configuration complexity and reduced reliability from adding another dependency, concluding that CDNs are commonly used even without caching.
quotes.key_insight: CDNs provide significant performance and reliability benefits beyond caching — particularly TLS termination and smart routing — but introduce real tradeoffs in security, configuration complexity, and dependency risk.

quotes.quote: I thought I knew how regular expressions worked on computers because I took a class on them in university, but it turns out that no, I did not know.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores how regular expressions are actually implemented on computers, sparked by an email about Google's RE2 and redgrep libraries. She contrasts the university textbook approach (converting regex to DFAs) with what actually happens in practice: Perl and friends use backtracking with exponential worst-case complexity, while Ken Thompson's 1960s algorithm avoids this blowup. She then introduces the surprising concept of 'regular expression derivatives' from Brzozowski's paper, where you can strip leading characters from a regex to produce a simpler regex. The redgrep library uses this derivative approach to compile regular expressions into LLVM bytecode via DFAs.
quotes.key_insight: Regular expression implementations in popular languages don't use the theoretically elegant DFA approach from textbooks, and there's a fascinating alternative using 'derivatives' of regular expressions that can compile them into efficient machine code.

quotes.quote: the important thing to know is: DFAs are simple and fast and very nice. they are state machines!

quotes.key_insight: Regular expression implementations in popular languages don't use the theoretically elegant DFA approach from textbooks, and there's a fascinating alternative using 'derivatives' of regular expressions that can compile them into efficient machine code.

quotes.quote: When Kamal told me about taking the derivative of a string I FREAKED OUT for like 10 seconds and I was like 'omg you can't take the derivative of a string! WHERE EVEN IS THE LIMIT. OMG.' But now I have calmed down and it seems cool.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores how regular expressions are actually implemented on computers, sparked by an email about Google's RE2 and redgrep libraries. She contrasts the university textbook approach (converting regex to DFAs) with what actually happens in practice: Perl and friends use backtracking with exponential worst-case complexity, while Ken Thompson's 1960s algorithm avoids this blowup. She then introduces the surprising concept of 'regular expression derivatives' from Brzozowski's paper, where you can strip leading characters from a regex to produce a simpler regex. The redgrep library uses this derivative approach to compile regular expressions into LLVM bytecode via DFAs.

quotes.quote: Basically every time I write a blog post, people reply with a huge amount of information about the topic; way more than I can take in immediately. This is great. Please do not stop doing that.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans compiles resources and insights about Java garbage collection that readers shared after her previous post on the topic. She highlights blog series, official Oracle docs, books, and informative Hacker News comments as valuable learning resources. A lengthy quoted comment provides practical advice on reducing GC pressure through mindful allocation patterns and library design. Evans also notes the existence of pauseless garbage collectors and the option to switch between different GC implementations in Java.
quotes.key_insight: Blogging about topics you're learning creates a feedback loop where readers contribute expert knowledge far beyond what you could gather alone, making the act of public learning itself a powerful research method.

quotes.quote: Yes, friends, I know this is news to absolutely nobody. But today I had up-close-and-personal problems with Java garbage collection for the first time so I am going to tell you about it.
quotes.summary: Evans recounts her first encounter with Java garbage collection performance problems, where a program slowed dramatically when processing 8 million lines instead of one million. Using the -XX:+PrintGCDetails JVM flag, she discovered that full GC collections were taking 1.5 seconds after only 0.2 seconds of work, effectively bringing the program to a halt. She explains the difference between fast young generation collections and slow full mark-and-sweep collections. The post introduces the concept of memory pressure: when an application uses memory close to its limit, constant garbage collection devastates performance. She concludes that sometimes the only fix is reducing memory allocation in the application itself.
quotes.key_insight: When a JVM application uses memory close to its limit, garbage collection can consume more time than actual computation, and the only real fix may be reducing how much memory the application allocates.

quotes.quote: Not understanding what my models are doing made me very uncomfortable.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans describes her discomfort with using machine learning models she doesn't understand and shares practical techniques for looking inside them. She contrasts simple logistic regression models (where you can read the coefficients) with large random forests that feel like black boxes. A colleague built a simple tool to show exactly which conditions each decision tree used for a given prediction, which immediately helped debug data quality issues and encoding mistakes. She argues that the common tradeoff framing of 'interpretability vs. performance' is a false choice, and that understanding what your models are doing is essential for making them better.
quotes.key_insight: You don't have to accept machine learning models as black boxes — even simple tools that show why a model made a specific decision can reveal bugs and dramatically improve your ability to build better models.

quotes.quote: People told me a lot 'well, you have a tradeoff between interpretability and how well the model performs!' But I didn't feel satisfied with that. I wanted interpretability *and* a model that performs well.

quotes.quote: What I love about all of these blogs (and what I strive for in my own writing) is that they all (at least sometimes) write about what they're currently working on or learning about.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares some of her favorite technical blogs and explains what makes each one special. She highlights Rachel by the Bay for sysadmin war stories, Dan Luu for hardware writing, Aphyr for distributed systems analysis, and Camille Fournier for engineering management reflections. The common thread she identifies is that all these bloggers write about what they're currently working on or learning, taking readers along for the ride. She closes with a wish for more people to write blogs sharing their ongoing work and thinking.
quotes.key_insight: The most valuable technical blogs are written by knowledgeable people who share what they're actively working on and learning, inviting readers along for the journey.

quotes.quote: I wanted to send a bunch of work to some worker threads, in parallel, have them do the work without anything exploding. That's it.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her journey to solve a seemingly simple concurrency problem: submitting work to a thread pool with backpressure so it blocks when overwhelmed, like Unix pipes. After discovering that Java's standard library doesn't offer this directly, she finds an elegant solution in the book 'Java Concurrency in Practice' using a semaphore-based BoundedExecutor pattern. The post captures her excitement at discovering semaphores as a concurrency primitive and celebrates the satisfaction of finally getting parallelization working correctly.
quotes.key_insight: The best concurrency model mirrors Unix pipes — a bounded buffer with backpressure — and a simple semaphore wrapper around a thread pool achieves exactly that.

quotes.quote: I thought a bit about how Unix pipes are one of my favorite abstractions. You pipe stuff from thing1 to thing2, it works, nothing explodes.

quotes.quote: Pipes have an internal buffer, and when the buffer is full, thing1 is not allowed to write to the pipe any more. It blocks.

quotes.quote: A semaphore is just a shared int that you can decrement and increment! acquire() decrements, release() increments. And if it gets to 0, semaphore.acquire() will block until someone else has released it.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her journey to solve a seemingly simple concurrency problem: submitting work to a thread pool with backpressure so it blocks when overwhelmed, like Unix pipes. After discovering that Java's standard library doesn't offer this directly, she finds an elegant solution in the book 'Java Concurrency in Practice' using a semaphore-based BoundedExecutor pattern. The post captures her excitement at discovering semaphores as a concurrency primitive and celebrates the satisfaction of finally getting parallelization working correctly.
quotes.key_insight: The best concurrency model mirrors Unix pipes — a bounded buffer with backpressure — and a simple semaphore wrapper around a thread pool achieves exactly that.

quotes.quote: Double.parseDouble in Java 7 is a synchronized method. So only one thread can parse doubles from strings at a time. Really? Really.
quotes.summary: Evans describes her struggle to parallelize a computation across 32 CPU cores in Scala/JVM, expecting it to be simple but encountering a cascade of concurrency problems. She discovered threads were blocked by Scala's lazy vals using synchronized internally and Java 7's synchronized Double.parseDouble method. After fixing blocking, she found the main thread couldn't feed work fast enough, then hit OutOfMemoryError from an unbounded work queue. The post uses this experience to reflect on how thread pool abstractions are leaky compared to truly great abstractions like TCP/IP or Unix pipes, which can be used for years without understanding their internals.
quotes.key_insight: Thread pool abstractions are fundamentally leaky — unlike great abstractions like TCP/IP, you cannot use them effectively without understanding the underlying implementation details like synchronization, work queues, and backpressure.

quotes.summary: Evans describes her struggle to parallelize a computation across 32 CPU cores in Scala/JVM, expecting it to be simple but encountering a cascade of concurrency problems. She discovered threads were blocked by Scala's lazy vals using synchronized internally and Java 7's synchronized Double.parseDouble method. After fixing blocking, she found the main thread couldn't feed work fast enough, then hit OutOfMemoryError from an unbounded work queue. The post uses this experience to reflect on how thread pool abstractions are leaky compared to truly great abstractions like TCP/IP or Unix pipes, which can be used for years without understanding their internals.
quotes.key_insight: Thread pool abstractions are fundamentally leaky — unlike great abstractions like TCP/IP, you cannot use them effectively without understanding the underlying implementation details like synchronization, work queues, and backpressure.

quotes.quote: I'd been querying this table, for months and it had been slow for months. Finally I was like "well, there has to be a reason why it's slow".
quotes.summary: Evans discovered why a Redshift query had been slow for months: a string column was taking up ~100GB on disk, requiring massive I/O on every query. Her coworker fixed it by enabling compression and changing the column from varchar(65536) to varchar(255). The post uses this as a reminder that cloud services are still bound by physical hardware constraints. Even 'magical' database-as-a-service products are just real computers with real disks and real I/O limits.
quotes.key_insight: Cloud services don't eliminate physical I/O constraints — understanding how your data is stored on disk matters just as much in the cloud as anywhere else.

quotes.quote: With these Magical Cloud Services it can sometimes be hard to remember that your data is just living on real computers somewhere, with real disks, and you're still bound by the normal limits of I/O.

quotes.quote: The first step when capacity planning is to figure out which resource(s) you're the most limited by, how much of that resource you're using right now, and how much capacity have you there.
quotes.summary: Evans explores the fundamentals of capacity planning, starting from identifying which resources (CPU, network, disk, databases) are bottlenecks during traffic spikes. She walks through a practical approach: observe failures, check graphs, identify the constrained resource, then measure usage. She discusses the subtlety of locks as a non-scalable resource and demonstrates through simulation how random load balancing creates uneven distribution across servers. The post concludes that measuring resource usage and reasoning about capacity is more effective than blindly adding servers.
quotes.key_insight: Capacity planning becomes far less mysterious when you systematically measure which specific resource is your bottleneck and reason about usage rather than reactively adding servers.

quotes.quote: The first time I ran tcpdump I took one look at some output like that, went WELP NOPE NOPE NOPE NOPE NOPE NOPE and gave up on tcpdump.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans makes the case that tcpdump is a powerful and approachable network debugging tool, despite its intimidating default output. She demonstrates a practical workflow: capture packets with tcpdump, then analyze them with Wireshark's friendly UI to diagnose problems like slow HTTP requests. She introduces pcap filter syntax for narrowing captures, discusses the overhead question for production use, and reveals tshark as a command-line tool that can decode application-layer protocols like HTTP and MongoDB. The post frames tcpdump mastery as achievable with just a few key techniques rather than deep TCP knowledge.
quotes.key_insight: tcpdump becomes a powerful and approachable debugging tool once you stop trying to read its raw output and instead capture packets to pcap files for analysis with friendlier tools like Wireshark and tshark.

quotes.quote: So it turns out I don't really know how perf works. And I like knowing how stuff works.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans investigates how the Linux perf profiling tool actually works by reading the kernel source code on a Saturday night. She discovers that hardware counters use the CPU's rdpmc instruction to provide exact counts of events like CPU instructions, with low enough overhead that the kernel runs them every context switch. For software event sampling, perf writes data to a mmap'd ring buffer in userspace, copying CPU register states at sample points. She traces the code path from the perf_event_open system call through core.c to the actual register-copying code in perf_regs.c, while honestly admitting she only understood about 10% of what she read.
quotes.key_insight: Reading kernel source code is surprisingly approachable — even understanding only 10% of what you read can teach you meaningful things about how your tools actually work.

quotes.quote: Imagine you have a program, and it has a pretty serious issue. It needs some deep architectural changes to fix it, but you can alleviate some of the symptoms by just changing a few lines of code.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans frames women-only tech spaces as a programming 'hack' — a quick fix that alleviates symptoms without addressing the root architectural problem. She explains that the core bug is women being treated badly at tech meetups, and excluding men is a simple way to prevent harassment. She acknowledges the downsides, including excluding allies and creating complexity around inclusion of trans and nonbinary people. She draws on her experience organizing Montreal All-Girl Hack Night and contrasts women-only spaces with alternative approaches like the Recurse Center's community-building model. The post argues these spaces aren't ideal but are pragmatically effective at making people feel safer.
quotes.key_insight: Women-only tech spaces are an imperfect but pragmatically effective workaround for harassment — a hack that alleviates symptoms while the deeper architectural problem remains unsolved.

quotes.quote: A HTTP library is conceptually a really simple thing – all HTTP is, for the most part, is you send some text to a server, and they send you some other text back.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans investigates Ruby HTTP client libraries and discovers surprising default behaviors hidden in commonly used dependencies. She finds that Net::HTTP automatically retries idempotent requests (GET, PUT, DELETE) without any way to disable it, following an HTTP/1.1 RFC recommendation that may not be appropriate for programmatic API clients. She also discovers that Excon splits HTTP request headers and body into separate TCP packets, causing performance problems related to Nagle's algorithm. The post advocates for actually reading the source code of your dependencies, noting that Net::HTTP is only about 2000 lines and reviewable. Evans approaches the discoveries with curiosity rather than blame, tracing each decision back to its rationale through open source archaeology.
quotes.key_insight: Dependencies hide surprising default behaviors that can cause real production problems, and the best defense is to actually read their source code and trace their design decisions back to their rationale.

quotes.quote: The scariest thing about introducing dependencies to me is – how do I know if it will surprise me unpleasantly a year from now, in production?
quotes.summary: Julia Evans investigates Ruby HTTP client libraries and discovers surprising default behaviors hidden in commonly used dependencies. She finds that Net::HTTP automatically retries idempotent requests (GET, PUT, DELETE) without any way to disable it, following an HTTP/1.1 RFC recommendation that may not be appropriate for programmatic API clients. She also discovers that Excon splits HTTP request headers and body into separate TCP packets, causing performance problems related to Nagle's algorithm. The post advocates for actually reading the source code of your dependencies, noting that Net::HTTP is only about 2000 lines and reviewable. quotes.quote: The basic question I want to answer here is – why do people sometimes discuss 'the stack' like it's some kind of revered fundamental object?
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores why 'the stack' is treated as a fundamental, almost revered concept in programming. Starting from basic call stacks in any language, she traces how C's call stack became the canonical One True Stack, examining the x86 assembly instructions (push, pop, callq, retq) that are built around specific assumptions about memory layout and the stack pointer register. She shows how deeply the stack format is baked into hardware instructions, then explores what happens when languages like Rust use different calling conventions. The post concludes that the stack really is fundamental because assembly instructions themselves encode assumptions about how it's organized.
quotes.key_insight: The stack isn't just a software abstraction — it's baked into hardware instructions, which is why it's treated as fundamental rather than as one of many possible choices.

quotes.quote: strace has not ever been able to solve all my problems – it only tells me about system calls, it can slow down my code up to 50x. Not ideal!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans introduces perf top as a simple but powerful tool for understanding CPU usage on Linux systems. She walks through three real-world debugging stories: identifying garbage collection issues in a Node program, discovering that encrypted swap was causing high kernel CPU usage, and finding that HTTPS connection overhead dominated a Python program's CPU time. The post demonstrates how perf top can quickly triage CPU problems even for programs written in high-level languages like Node, Python, or Java. She positions perf top as an essential addition to any developer's debugging toolbox alongside strace.
quotes.key_insight: perf top is a simple one-command tool that can immediately reveal what's consuming CPU at the function level, making it invaluable for quickly triaging performance problems even in high-level language programs.

quotes.quote: If you have a slow program, the first thing you want to know is whether your program is spending that time calculating things on the CPU, or whether it's waiting for something else (a disk, a network, user input).
quotes.summary: The post explains how to use Linux's clock_gettime system call to measure CPU time spent by a process or thread. Evans discovered this after a colleague mentioned measuring CPU time per HTTP request at work. The key insight is that clock_gettime with the CLOCK_PROCESS_CPUTIME_ID flag returns actual CPU time consumed, not wall-clock time, letting you distinguish between time spent computing and time spent waiting. She walks through the different clock flags available and shows how to bracket code with clock_gettime calls to measure CPU consumption.
quotes.key_insight: Linux's clock_gettime system call with CLOCK_PROCESS_CPUTIME_ID lets you measure actual CPU time consumed by your code, distinguishing computation from waiting — and it's accessible from any language, not just C.

quotes.quote: Sampling profilers, the most common performance debugging tool, are notoriously bad at debugging problems caused by tail latency because they aggregate events into averages. But tail latency is, by definition, not average.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans argues that log files are an underrated debugging tool, especially for diagnosing tail latency problems that sampling profilers and dashboards miss. Inspired by Dan Luu's post on the limitations of sampling profilers, she shares how looking at raw logs for slow requests immediately revealed the source of a performance problem that graphs couldn't surface. She explains that while graphs are great for overall trends and correlations, logs excel at tracing individual slow requests to pinpoint exactly where time is being spent. She connects this to the broader insight that tracing tools, from simple print statements to sophisticated distributed tracing systems like Zipkin, all share the same fundamental advantage over aggregated metrics.
quotes.key_insight: Log files beat dashboards and profilers for diagnosing tail latency because they preserve the individual event traces that aggregated metrics hide.

quotes.quote: if you have 1000 events that were unusually slow, they probably failed because of a relatively uncommon event. And uncommon events do not always show up in graphs. But know where they show up? LOG FILES. WHICH ARE THE BEST.
