quotes.quote: When I first read that I thought it was a typo. Surely nothing can do a million anythings per second? It wasn't a typo.
quotes.summary: Evans argues that developers should build intuition about how fast computers actually are, rather than accepting slow performance as inevitable. She walks through concrete benchmarks showing nginx can handle 1M QPS, Java servers can do 50K QPS, and even toy Python servers can manage 1K QPS. She then catalogs common reasons servers underperform (CPU saturation, worker starvation, network limits, database load, disk I/O) and argues that understanding these bottlenecks requires investigation, not assumptions. Her core message is that developing an 'absolute sense' of computer speed helps you identify when something is unreasonably slow and gives you confidence to invest time in profiling and optimization.
quotes.key_insight: Building concrete intuition about how fast computers actually are — not just relative comparisons — gives you the confidence and ability to diagnose and fix real performance problems.

quotes.quote: These are very interesting to read as a distributed systems n00b – the authors of the articles are making significantly different arguments, and so it's a useful exercise to try to reason through the arguments and figure out who you think is right.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her learning experience about clock skew in distributed systems, prompted by reading the debate between Martin Kleppmann and antirez about Redis's Redlock distributed locking system. She walks through Kleppmann's arguments about why distributed locks can fail—process pauses, network delays, and unreliable clocks—and admits she initially found clock skew hard to believe since she'd never personally encountered it. After asking distributed systems experts on Twitter, she learned that clocks running at wrong speeds is a real and well-documented problem, not just a theoretical concern.
quotes.key_insight: Clock skew is a real operational problem in distributed systems, not just a theoretical edge case, and reasoning through expert debates is a valuable way to build understanding of distributed systems challenges.

quotes.quote: CPU load averages have long been a little mysterious to me. I understood that low is good, and high is bad, but I thought of them as a mostly inscrutable number.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explains CPU load averages by connecting them to practical web server performance analysis through Little's Law from queueing theory. She shows that dividing the load average by request throughput gives you the average CPU time per request, a formula she found useful for real-world debugging. The post walks through what load averages actually measure (processes in running or runnable state), then derives the formula and lists important caveats, including Linux's inclusion of uninterruptible sleep processes. She also proposes an extension for estimating actual on-CPU time versus waiting time when CPUs are overloaded.
quotes.key_insight: Little's Law from queueing theory turns the seemingly opaque CPU load average into a practical debugging tool: divide it by your request throughput to get average CPU time per request.

quotes.quote: So the idea is – this pattern of reading a file and writing to a socket is really common! So they made a system call to just do that!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores the sendfile system call, explaining how it optimizes the common pattern of reading a file and writing it to a network socket by eliminating unnecessary data copies and context switches. She presents impressive performance benchmarks, including a Python FTP library doubling throughput and Netflix going from 6Gbps to 40Gbps on FreeBSD. However, she also covers the pitfalls, including a serious OS X bug that caused 5-second delays. The post connects sendfile to related system calls like splice and tee, and discusses practical configuration options in web servers like nginx and Apache.
quotes.key_insight: The sendfile system call can dramatically improve file-serving performance by keeping data in kernel space, but its platform-specific bugs remind us that even performance optimizations need to be tested and configurable.

quotes.quote: One of the big takeaways for me in this talk was 'whoa, hashsets are not fast. okay.'
quotes.summary: Evans recounts a Papers We Love meetup talk by Daniel Lemire about roaring bitmaps, a data structure for fast integer set operations. She explains why integer sets matter for databases (row ID intersections for queries), introduces bitmaps as a compact alternative to hash sets, and dives into how modern CPUs can execute multiple instructions per cycle. The post connects CPU architecture concepts like instruction pipelines and SIMD to why roaring bitmaps are designed the way they are. She uses perf stat to demonstrate instructions-per-cycle differences between programs, showing that simple bitwise operations parallelize well at the CPU level.
quotes.key_insight: Fast integer set operations matter for real systems like databases, and designing data structures that align with how modern CPUs actually work (instruction-level parallelism, SIMD) can yield massive performance gains beyond what naive implementations achieve.

quotes.quote: But WHAT FILE DID IT READ? This is not good at all.
quotes.summary: Julia explores ftrace, a Linux kernel tracing tool, by trying to figure out which CPU registers hold system call arguments. Starting with Brendan Gregg's kprobe tool, she traces the read system call but initially can't see which file is being read. By printing all register values and cross-referencing with strace output, she deduces which registers hold the file descriptor and read length. She discovers this detective work makes ftrace usable for low-overhead system call tracing, and later learns there's actually a standard table mapping registers to system call arguments.
quotes.key_insight: You can figure out Linux kernel internals through experimentation and cross-referencing tools, turning seemingly wizardly knowledge into a systematic process of elimination.

quotes.quote: calling C code is definitely unsafe. it could be doing ANYTHING
quotes.summary: Julia documents her experience learning to call C functions from Rust, working through compiler errors with help from a friend. She shows a minimal example of using Rust's FFI to call strcpy and puts from C, wrapping them in an unsafe block. She also notes how easy cargo makes it to start new Rust projects, and that Rust installation has improved since 2013 though error messages remain challenging.
quotes.key_insight: Calling C from Rust is straightforward once you understand extern declarations and unsafe blocks, but Rust's strict type system makes even simple FFI tasks a learning experience.

quotes.quote: on Linux, learn strace. It's the best.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares follow-up notes and resources from her CUSEC conference talk. The post covers two main topics from the talk: debugging techniques and understanding computer performance. She recommends specific tools like strace, dtrace, wireshark, and mitmproxy for debugging, and profilers like visualvm and YourKit for performance analysis. The post serves as a curated reference list linking to her own blog posts and external resources.
quotes.key_insight: Debugging and performance analysis become approachable when you know the right tools — strace, dtrace, wireshark, and profilers — and have concrete stories showing how they solve real problems.

quotes.quote: It turns out that talking about confusing equity / compensation issues in public channels at work is awesome.
quotes.summary: After writing a blog post about misunderstanding stock options, Julia Evans started asking questions about equity compensation in a public Slack channel at work. The discussion became so popular that a quarter of the company joined a dedicated #equity channel within days. She discovered that open discussion of confusing compensation topics benefits everyone because it reveals that nobody fully understands these issues. The post advocates for transparency around equity compensation in the workplace.
quotes.key_insight: Openly discussing confusing compensation topics at work helps everyone because it surfaces shared ignorance and creates space for collective learning.

quotes.quote: I used to write Java at work, and my Java programs were really slow. I thought (and people sometimes told me) that this was because Java is a slow programming language.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans challenges the common misconception that Java is inherently slow, arguing that the language itself is capable of extremely high performance. She points to examples like the LMAX Disruptor processing 6 million events per second and databases like Cassandra written in Java. She acknowledges her own Java code was slow due to excessive object allocation and garbage collection pressure, not the language itself. The post highlights a thriving ecosystem of high-performance Java libraries and recommends VisualVM as a free, easy first step to profiling slow JVM code.
quotes.key_insight: Java isn't a slow language — slow Java code is usually caused by patterns like excessive object allocation, and profiling with free tools like VisualVM is the first step to fixing it.

quotes.quote: Everything was 'well it works in practice' and so little of it was math. I was a pure math major at the time, so arguments like 'well it works in practice' made me REALLY MAD.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explains the bias-variance decomposition, a mathematical framework that clarifies why machine learning techniques like ensembles and regularization actually work. Coming from a pure math background, she was frustrated by machine learning's reliance on heuristics rather than theory. The bias-variance decomposition theorem shows that prediction error decomposes into bias squared plus variance plus irreducible error, which provides actionable insight for model building. She carefully labels which parts of her explanation are rigorous math versus practical heuristics. The post demonstrates how ensembles reduce variance by averaging correlated models, and how regularization trades increased bias for decreased variance. Throughout, she maintains intellectual honesty about where the math ends and the hand-waving begins.
quotes.key_insight: The bias-variance decomposition is one of the few pieces of actual math in machine learning that explains why techniques like ensembles and regularization reduce error, turning a supposed tradeoff into something you can win.

quotes.quote: Scenario #2 is a disaster. It took me a long time to understand why, but I think the primary reason is that it's very hard to interrupt and indicate that I want to speak when I'm remote.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares practical strategies for participating in meetings as a remote worker. She identifies two key scenarios: all-remote meetings (which work fine) and mixed meetings with mostly local participants (which are much harder for the remote person). The core problem is that remote participants can't easily interrupt or signal they want to speak. She describes learning to be deliberately loud and explicit about wanting to talk, and also notes that local participants can actively help by soliciting input from remote attendees.
quotes.key_insight: Mixed local-remote meetings systematically silence remote participants, and the fix requires both remote people learning to be deliberately explicit about turn-taking and local people actively soliciting remote input.

quotes.quote: Stock options are BORING AND COMPLICATED AND AWFUL. They are full of taxes, which we all know are awful.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her personal experience of accepting a job offer with stock options she didn't understand, and walks through the math that reveals how expensive and risky exercising options can be. She explains that when you leave a private company, you typically have only 3 months to exercise your options, which can cost hundreds of thousands of dollars in exercise prices plus taxes on unrealized gains. Using both a hypothetical example and her own situation at Stripe, she shows how the total cost of exercising can exceed an employee's entire savings. The post provides a concrete list of basic and advanced questions to ask before accepting an offer, covering everything from exercise prices to liquidation preferences and early exercise options.
quotes.key_insight: Stock options at private companies can be effectively worthless to employees who can't afford the exercise price and tax bill when they leave, so you should do the math before accepting an offer rather than after.

quotes.quote: I was thinking of trying to sell it but it felt like too much work. Instead I spent maybe $300 on printing and gave it away for free, since I have a job.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her 2015, highlighting the creation of her first strace zine, giving talks at PyCon, blogging about systems programming and performance, and a successful machine learning project at work. She also candidly discusses dealing with wrist pain from typing all year, which forced her to pivot to drawing comics and be more intentional about what she spends her limited typing energy on. The post balances celebration of creative output with honest acknowledgment of physical limitations, concluding that constraints can sharpen focus on what truly matters.
quotes.key_insight: Physical constraints can sharpen creative focus — when every hour of typing costs something, you stop wasting time on work that doesn't matter.

quotes.key_insight: Physical constraints can sharpen creative focus — when every hour of typing costs something, you stop wasting time on work that doesn't matter.

quotes.quote: I actually liked it a lot more than an industry conference because everything was so alien & unfamiliar to me and everyone knew way more about their field than me.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recaps her experience at the Women in Machine Learning 2015 conference (part of NIPS), expressing enthusiasm for attending academic conferences outside her usual domain. She highlights three talks: Lillian Lee's research on how tweet phrasing affects retweets, Been Kim's work on interpretable ML and human-machine interaction, and Corinna Cortes' talk on web scraping for fact discovery at Google. A recurring theme is her appreciation for interpretable, debuggable ML and for speakers who are honest about how hard ML really is rather than pretending it's magic.
quotes.key_insight: The most valuable machine learning work focuses on interpretability and helping humans do better work, not on replacing them or pretending the results come easily.

quotes.quote: Nobody writes code to defend against an exception being raised on literally any line. That's not even possible.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explains why Ruby's Timeout module is dangerous after encountering a difficult bug at work caused by it. The core issue is that Timeout uses Thread.raise to inject an exception into a running thread at any arbitrary point in execution, meaning the exception can fire during cleanup code, rescue blocks, or any line of code whatsoever. She surveys how other languages handle the same problem—Java deprecated and then disabled Thread.stop, C# discourages Thread.Abort, and C++ threads aren't interruptible at all. The fundamental flaw isn't Ruby-specific: there is no way to safely interrupt an arbitrary block of code. Java's Thread.interrupt offers a better model by only allowing interruption at specific safe points.
quotes.key_insight: There is no way to safely interrupt an arbitrary block of code because an exception injected at any random line can corrupt state during cleanup, rescue blocks, or any other operation.

quotes.quote: Everything on a computer does in fact happen for a logical reason (however much the computer may try to convince you otherwise).
quotes.summary: Julia Evans reflects on how she became good at debugging after receiving positive feedback in a performance review. She identifies several key factors: maintaining confidence that bugs have logical causes, building broad systems knowledge, talking through problems with others, and mastering observability tools like strace and tcpdump. She notes that debugging skill came not from being systematic and organized, but from persistence, knowledge accumulation, and learning to enjoy the challenge. The post concludes with her realization that difficult bugs are now more exciting to her than writing new code.
quotes.key_insight: Debugging skill comes from combining confidence that bugs have logical causes, deep systems knowledge, willingness to talk through problems, and mastery of observability tools — and getting better at it makes it genuinely enjoyable.

quotes.quote: It really should not take 40 milliseconds to send a HTTP request to localhost. Something was terribly wrong.
quotes.summary: Evans argues that understanding TCP basics is practically essential for any developer, not just systems programmers. She illustrates this through a real debugging story where HTTP requests to a local queue were inexplicably taking 40ms each. The root cause turned out to be the interaction between Nagle's algorithm and TCP delayed ACKs, which creates passive waiting between sender and receiver. By setting TCP_NODELAY, the delays instantly disappeared. She references John Nagle himself calling delayed ACKs a bad idea from 1985 that TCP continues to use even when it consistently loses its bet. The post demonstrates how a small amount of TCP knowledge can turn a mysterious week-long debugging puzzle into a simple fix.
quotes.key_insight: A small amount of TCP knowledge can turn mysterious performance bugs into instant fixes, because protocol-level issues like Nagle's algorithm interacting with delayed ACKs affect anyone making HTTP requests, not just systems programmers.

quotes.quote: That period where the application and HAProxy are both passive-aggressively waiting for the other to send information? That's the extra 200ms.

quotes.quote: I didn't really understand what Docker was *for* until yesterday. I mean, containers. Cool. Whatever.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans describes her sudden appreciation for Docker after struggling to set up a complex C++ environment for neural network experiments. She needed Ubuntu 14.04 with many dependencies and was dreading reinstalling her OS until she remembered Docker could give her an isolated environment instantly. She highlights Docker's speed compared to VMs, the reproducibility of Dockerfiles, and the freedom to make messy configurations without risking her main system. The post captures the 'aha moment' of understanding Docker's practical value through a real problem.
quotes.key_insight: Docker's real value clicks when you need an isolated, reproducible environment for messy dependency chains — it turns an unreproducible mess into a reproducible one.

quotes.quote: The core idea in this paper is – if you have a line of code in a thread, and you want to know if it's making your program slow, speed up that line of code to see if it makes the whole program faster!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans enthusiastically reviews the COZ paper on causal profiling, a technique for profiling multithreaded programs. Traditional profiling breaks down with threads because speeding up one thread may not help if another thread is the bottleneck. COZ's core insight is to simulate speeding up a line of code by slowing down all other threads, using Linux perf without modifying the program. The technique was validated on real programs like SQLite and Memcached, finding issues like hash table collisions and unnecessary locking. Evans also speculates about applying causal profiling to single-threaded async applications.
quotes.key_insight: Causal profiling solves the hard problem of profiling threaded programs by simulating speedups through slowing down all other threads, revealing which code actually matters for overall performance.

quotes.quote: Of course, you can't actually speed up a thread. But you can slow down all other threads!

The technique was validated on real programs like SQLite and Memcached, finding issues like hash table collisions and unnecessary locking. Evans also speculates about applying causal profiling to single-threaded async applications.
quotes.key_insight: Causal profiling solves the hard problem of profiling threaded programs by simulating speedups through slowing down all other threads, revealing which code actually matters for overall performance.

quotes.quote: They also find out places where speeding up a line of code would introduce a slowdown (because of increased contention around some resource).
quotes.summary: Julia Evans enthusiastically reviews the COZ paper on causal profiling, a technique for profiling multithreaded programs. Traditional profiling breaks down with threads because speeding up one thread may not help if another thread is the bottleneck. COZ's core insight is to simulate speeding up a line of code by slowing down all other threads, using Linux perf without modifying the program. The technique was validated on real programs like SQLite and Memcached, finding issues like hash table collisions and unnecessary locking. Evans also speculates about applying causal profiling to single-threaded async applications.
quotes.key_insight: Causal profiling solves the hard problem of profiling threaded programs by simulating speedups through slowing down all other threads, revealing which code actually matters for overall performance.

quotes.quote: I used to think a millisecond was fast.
quotes.summary: Evans describes profiling a slow machine learning pipeline at Stripe where processing 6 million records took 1.5 hours. Using VisualVM, she and colleagues traced the bottleneck to a single Math.pow() call inside a HyperLogLog estimation loop in Twitter's Algebird library. By replacing the expensive floating-point power function with a bit shift operation or lookup table, they achieved a 100x speedup. The post emphasizes building intuition for when code is unreasonably slow by knowing baseline performance expectations for simple operations.
quotes.key_insight: Developing intuition for expected performance baselines — knowing that 10,000 simple CPU operations should take microseconds, not milliseconds — is the key skill that turns vague slowness into targeted, dramatic fixes.

quotes.quote: Being responsible for the accuracy of a system that you don't fully understand is scary/ very interesting.
quotes.summary: Evans reflects on the safety and responsibility implications of using machine learning in production systems. She distinguishes between simple, interpretable models and complex ones like random forests, noting that complex models can perform well but make unpredictable mistakes. She argues that being responsible for a system you don't fully understand is both scary and interesting. The post offers practical mitigation strategies like human review, matching model complexity to error tolerance, and using ML insights to improve simpler systems.
quotes.key_insight: The responsible use of machine learning requires matching model complexity to your tolerance for unpredictable mistakes, and using human oversight and interpretability as safety nets.

quotes.quote: A model you don't understand is awesome. It can perform really well, and you can save time at first by ignoring the details.

quotes.quote: I analyze data, and I rarely think about what the underlying distribution of my data is.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explains bootstrap confidence intervals as a practical, non-parametric approach to quantifying uncertainty in statistical estimates. Using a playful airline overbooking scenario, she demonstrates how sampling with replacement from your data thousands of times can produce useful confidence intervals without needing to know the underlying distribution. She shows that with only 8 data points, bootstrapping reveals wide uncertainty, while 1000 data points yield much tighter estimates. She acknowledges skipping the mathematical justification for why bootstrapping works but emphasizes its practical value as a go-to method for getting error bars.
quotes.key_insight: Bootstrap confidence intervals give you a practical, distribution-free way to understand how much you should trust a statistical estimate, using nothing but resampling and computation.

quotes.quote: I didn't know there were people who specialized in testing software and were really awesome at it who don't write programs!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recaps her experience at AdaCamp Montreal 2015, an unconference for women in open technology and culture. She highlights discovering the specialized role of software testers who don't write code, meeting a diverse range of fascinating people, having productive discussions about codes of conduct, and learning about effective moderation techniques for group discussions. The post reflects on how unconference formats enable unexpected, valuable conversations and how voicing your specific interests often reveals others share them.
quotes.key_insight: Voicing your specific interests and concerns in collaborative settings usually reveals that others share them, and unconference formats create the space for those unexpected connections.

quotes.quote: I wrote a zine to teach people about how to debug their programs using strace
quotes.summary: Julia Evans announces the release of her strace zine, a printable guide teaching people how to debug programs using strace. Originally planned as a mailed zine, she instead made it available as a free PDF download. The post provides practical printing instructions for making physical copies and encourages readers to share it with friends, colleagues, and students.
quotes.key_insight: Technical knowledge about debugging tools like strace can be made accessible and shareable through simple, printable zine formats.

quotes.quote: Contributing to a project for the first time is hard. There's a lot you need to know!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans proposes reframing open source sprints as learning opportunities rather than contribution obligations. She observes that contributing to a complex project for the first time is genuinely hard, and the pressure to produce a contribution in a few days can make sprints unproductive. Instead, she suggests picking a project, deciding what you want to learn about it, and digging in without worrying about making a contribution. This approach might actually lead to better contributions later, once you understand the project's internals.
quotes.key_insight: Open source sprints become more valuable when you reframe them as learning opportunities rather than feeling obligated to produce a contribution.

quotes.quote: netstat tells me the truth about whether it is running.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans surveys several Linux tools beyond strace for understanding what your operating system is doing. She covers netstat for checking open ports, dstat for real-time disk and network stats, lsof for tracking open files, ngrep/tcpdump for network packet inspection, opensnoop/ftrace for file access tracing, and atop for comprehensive process monitoring. Each tool is presented as a way to discover 'The Truth' about what's happening on your system. She emphasizes that these tools are valuable because they give you direct, unmediated answers to specific questions about your computer's behavior.
quotes.key_insight: Linux provides a rich ecosystem of simple, focused tools that tell you the truth about what your system is actually doing, and learning them is best driven by real debugging stories rather than man pages alone.

quotes.quote: You all know by now that I'm pretty into strace, but – strace is not always a good choice! If your program runs too many system calls, strace will slow it down. A lot.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores using perf as an alternative to strace for observing system calls, motivated by strace's significant performance overhead. She demonstrates that strace slowed du by 65x due to 260,000 system calls, while perf record can capture the same information with minimal impact. She walks through the basic perf record and perf script commands but notes a limitation: perf shows memory addresses for filenames rather than the actual strings. The post ends with a call for help from readers on resolving this limitation.
quotes.key_insight: When strace's performance overhead is unacceptable for syscall-heavy programs, perf's tracepoint-based recording offers a low-overhead alternative for observing system calls, though with less convenient output.

quotes.quote: It's about how fortune tellers do not necessarily also need to be cattle wranglers.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans describes different archetypes of senior engineers using fantasy hero metaphors. She identifies four distinct roles: the fortune teller who predicts engineering problems, the cattle wrangler who builds consensus, the spring of knowledge who holds deep technical expertise, and the gardener who helps manage technical debt. The post argues that senior engineering excellence takes many different forms, and that these specializations don't necessarily overlap. She references Rent the Runway's engineering ladder as a related framework that maps engineering qualities to RPG attributes.
quotes.key_insight: Senior engineering excellence comes in distinct archetypes — prediction, consensus-building, deep expertise, and code stewardship — and no one person needs to embody all of them.

quotes.quote: I specifically wanted programming-language-independent ways to investigate questions like this, and I guess people who follow me on twitter get me because I got SO MANY GREAT ANSWERS.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans presents a detective-style investigation into diagnosing why programs are slow, using three mystery programs as case studies. She demonstrates how to distinguish between CPU-bound programs, those waiting on network responses, and those doing heavy disk I/O using language-independent OS tools. The first mystery is solved using `ps` with `wchan` to reveal network socket waiting, the second is identified as CPU-bound via `time`, and the third reveals surprising filesystem cache behavior where writes appear fast but are deferred by the kernel. The disk I/O case is the most interesting, showing how consecutive runs take dramatically different times due to the kernel's write caching filling up. She also compiles a comprehensive list of Linux performance tools recommended by her Twitter followers.
quotes.key_insight: A handful of OS-level tools like `time`, `ps -eo wchan`, and `dstat` can quickly diagnose whether a slow program is CPU-bound, network-bound, or IO-bound without ever reading the source code.

quotes.quote: We have a choice about who submits talk proposals, and also about who submits the *best* talk proposals.
quotes.summary: Evans challenges conference organizers who claim they have no control over the diversity of their talk submissions. She argues that active outreach to underrepresented groups, combined with helping people improve their proposals, directly increases both the quantity and quality of diverse submissions. She cites PyCon's dramatic increase in women speakers (from 1% to 33% over four years) as proof that deliberate outreach works. The post frames diversity in conference lineups as a choice organizers actively make or fail to make.
quotes.key_insight: Conference diversity isn't a matter of luck or pipeline — it's the direct result of organizers choosing to do personal outreach and help underrepresented speakers craft strong proposals.

quotes.quote: What's happening now that I would like to not be happening in a month?
quotes.summary: Julia Evans compiles crowd-sourced advice from Twitter on what to discuss during one-on-one meetings with your manager. The post lists specific questions covering promotions, team dynamics, learning, career trajectory, and project satisfaction. Beyond topic ideas, she shares meta-advice on how to approach 1:1s more effectively, like keeping running lists of discussion items throughout the week. The post emphasizes that 1:1s should be reserved for things you wouldn't say in front of all your coworkers.
quotes.key_insight: The most valuable 1:1 discussions are the ones you wouldn't have in front of the whole team, and keeping a running list throughout the week ensures you don't forget them.

quotes.quote: But I always somehow thought of the locate database as this Complicated Thing. Until today I started looking at it!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans investigates how the locate command works by examining its database file, discovering it's essentially a compressed recursive directory listing. She builds a homegrown alternative by piping find's output to a text file and searching it with grep, and finds that this naive approach is actually faster than locate despite using 3x more disk space. She traces this back to a 1983 paper that justified locate's compressed format based on constraints—slow grep over 20,000 files, limited disk space—that no longer apply with modern hardware. The post questions whether other Unix tools carry similar outdated design assumptions.
quotes.key_insight: The locate command's compressed database format was optimized for 1983 hardware constraints that no longer exist, and a naive grep over a plain text file list is actually faster on modern systems—raising the question of what other Unix tools carry outdated design assumptions.

quotes.quote: none of these codes is a prefix of any other code. So if we write down 010001001011 we can see that it's 010 00 1001 011 or baec! There wasn't any ambiguity
quotes.summary: This post explains the second half of how gzip compression works: Huffman coding for individual characters. After gzip uses LZ77 to eliminate repetitions in text, it uses Huffman codes to represent frequently-occurring characters with fewer bits. Evans walks through how prefix-free codes work, demonstrates reading real Huffman tables from compressed files using the infgen tool, and shows how the gzip spec converts code lengths into actual Huffman codes. The post connects theoretical computer science concepts to their practical application in everyday tools.
quotes.key_insight: Gzip's Huffman coding assigns shorter bit sequences to more frequent characters, and you can reconstruct the full coding table from just the code lengths using the algorithm in the gzip specification.

quotes.quote: You might ALSO notice that these are all less than 8 bits! That means we're doing COMPRESSION.
quotes.summary: This post explains the second half of how gzip compression works: Huffman coding for individual characters. After gzip uses LZ77 to eliminate repetitions in text, it uses Huffman codes to represent frequently-occurring characters with fewer bits. Evans walks through how prefix-free codes work, demonstrates reading real Huffman tables from compressed files using the infgen tool, and shows how the gzip spec converts code lengths into actual Huffman codes. The post connects theoretical computer science concepts to their practical application in everyday tools.

quotes.quote: I don't generally like giving advice, because I think it's way too easy to generalize your own experience into advice and do it wrong.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her decade-long journey learning to program, pushing back against the notion that programming can be learned quickly. She traces her path from writing BASIC on a TI-83 calculator in 2004 through university and into professional work, noting that progress was slow and nonlinear. Looking back at her old code, she was surprised not by its quality but by how little she had actually written in those early years. The post serves as reassurance to beginners that learning to program is a long process, and that even experienced developers spent years feeling uncertain about their abilities.
quotes.key_insight: Learning to program is a years-long journey of slow, uneven progress, and the pressure to learn quickly in weeks or months doesn't match the reality of how most programmers actually develop their skills.

quotes.quote: I spent about 10 years learning to program before going to Hacker School, mostly at fairly low speeds.

quotes.quote: I'd never heard of A/A testing before, didn't really understand what she meant at the time, and didn't manage to ask. (pro tip: ask questions when you have them :))
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explains A/A testing, a technique she learned at a Data Science meetup from an Airbnb talk about common experimentation mistakes. The concept involves comparing two control groups (instead of a treatment and control) to gauge how much natural variation exists in your data. By seeing how much difference appears between two identical groups, you can get an intuitive sense of whether your experimental results are meaningful or just noise. She walks through a widget store example showing how a promising 2% sales increase looks less impressive when the control-vs-control comparison shows similar variation. The technique is formally called A/A/B testing when combined with an experimental group.
quotes.key_insight: Comparing two control groups against each other reveals the natural noise level in your data, giving you an intuitive baseline to judge whether your experimental results are real or just random variation.

quotes.quote: I understood before seeing this talk that doing database upgrades was hard, and that it's appropriate to be incredibly skeptical, but I didn't have any clue how to plan to reduce your uncertainty so that you can actually do the upgrade.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recaps her experience at Data Day Texas 2015, a one-day conference where she attended talks on database upgrades, human-in-the-loop machine learning, and generating fake datasets. Her favorite talk was Charity Majors' presentation on upgrading MongoDB at scale at Parse, which gave her a new appreciation for the planning and testing required for database operations. She also highlights Robert Munro's talk on using human input to improve ML classifiers, particularly the psychological aspects of keeping human labelers engaged. The post reflects her enthusiasm for talks that genuinely advance her understanding of a topic.
quotes.key_insight: Reducing uncertainty in risky operations like database upgrades requires systematic, patient testing against real production workloads — not just hoping for the best.

quotes.quote: I like to think of this as a 'oh right I actually need to know how to program' moment – nobody's going to tell me what to do, I just need to figure it out.
quotes.summary: Evans reflects on learning to read source code directly instead of relying solely on documentation, a skill she picked up at her first programming job working with Drupal. She describes how reading source code works well for specific detail questions but breaks down when you're missing fundamental conceptual understanding. She proposes a hierarchy of bug difficulty ranging from immediately obvious problems to ones requiring bigger-picture knowledge you don't even know you're missing. For that hardest category, she advocates asking knowledgeable colleagues as the best strategy.
quotes.key_insight: Reading source code is a powerful debugging skill, but it only works when you have enough conceptual understanding of the system — without that bigger picture, you need to ask people who have it.

quotes.quote: I didn't grow up with the idea that I was worse at math or programming than the men around me (because, well, I wasn't!)
quotes.summary: Julia Evans addresses the recurring experience of being misgendered in online comments about her technical blog posts, where readers default to 'he' when discussing her work. She reflects on how she never internalized the idea that programming was a male domain, finding the assumption that technical content must be written by a man simply silly. She asserts her sense of belonging in the programming community and pushes back against gatekeeping of all kinds. The post concludes with a broad, inclusive declaration that programming belongs to anyone who writes programs.
quotes.key_insight: The programming community belongs to everyone who programs, and the default assumption that technical work is produced by men reveals an illegitimate sense of ownership over who gets to participate.

quotes.quote: I feel like the community belongs to me, and like I'm a part of it.

quotes.quote: Fear creates local maximums.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores how fear in programming leads to overly conservative decisions and worse software. She distinguishes between healthy caution and paralyzing fear that prevents necessary changes like big refactors or deploys. The post argues that better tools and processes—version control, testing, CI, canary deploys—reduce fear by providing safety nets. Beyond tooling, she emphasizes that blameless postmortems and a culture that doesn't punish mistakes are essential for enabling bold improvements. She also notes that fears can be irrational and contagious, persisting even after objective risks are mitigated.
quotes.key_insight: Fear of breaking things creates local maximums in software quality—investing in safety nets like tests, deploys, and blameless culture converts paralyzing fear into productive caution.

quotes.quote: If you're scared of making changes, you can't make something dramatically better, or do that big code cleanup.

quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores how fear in programming leads to overly conservative decisions and worse software. She distinguishes between healthy caution and paralyzing fear that prevents necessary changes like big refactors or deploys. The post argues that better tools and processes—version control, testing, CI, canary deploys—reduce fear by providing safety nets. Beyond tooling, she emphasizes that blameless postmortems and a culture that doesn't punish mistakes are essential for enabling bold improvements. She also notes that fears can be irrational and contagious, persisting even after objective risks are mitigated.
quotes.key_insight: Fear of breaking things creates local maximums in software quality—investing in safety nets like tests, deploys, and blameless culture converts paralyzing fear into productive caution.

quotes.quote: Even with good design, w/o tests you will fear change and so the code will rot. With good tests, there's no fear, so you'll clean the code.

quotes.quote: Instead of being all 'wow that person is so great they must be a genius and a wizard', it's better to ask 'okay, what specifically do I admire about that person? Is that a way I want to be? How did they get like that? Can I do that too?'
quotes.summary: Evans reflects on how to think about people we admire, arguing that instead of putting them on a pedestal, we should identify specific qualities we respect and ask whether we can develop those same qualities ourselves. She gives examples like admiring Terence Tao's public writing about math without needing to become a mathematician, and learning from a friend's skill at giving thoughtful feedback. She concludes that idolizing people is both unfair to ourselves and awkward for them, and adds an important caveat about the privilege that makes it easier for some people to pursue what they admire.
quotes.key_insight: Instead of idolizing people as unreachable geniuses, identify the specific qualities you admire and deliberately practice developing those qualities yourself.

quotes.quote: Demystifying and writing about things I find interesting in public are things I can do without trying to win the Fields medal

quotes.quote: Actually understanding a Thing is hard if I've never done it.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her first experience writing threaded programs, starting with a deliberately buggy race condition in C and then fixing it using mutexes and atomics in both C and Rust. She discovers that mutexes carry a significant performance penalty (0.08s to 3.1s) and that Rust's mutex implementation was even slower due to its channel-based approach. She then explores atomic operations as a faster alternative, using perf and flame graphs to understand performance differences. The post traces the journey from abstract knowledge of concurrency concepts to hands-on understanding of the actual CPU instructions (the x86 LOCK prefix) that make thread-safe operations work.
quotes.key_insight: Hands-on experimentation with threading reveals that atomic operations can be orders of magnitude faster than mutexes because they use hardware-level CPU instructions instead of expensive kernel system calls.

quotes.quote: So when the people at Big Data Montreal asked if I wanted to give a talk about stracing Hadoop, the answer was YES OBVIOUSLY.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans demonstrates how to use strace to observe the internal workings of Hadoop's distributed filesystem (HDFS). She sets up a small Hadoop cluster and traces the system calls made when reading a file, revealing the two-phase process: first contacting the namenode to get block locations, then connecting to datanodes to retrieve the actual data. She decodes the Protocol Buffer messages in the strace output and locates the physical block files on the datanode's filesystem. The post culminates in a live demo discovery where she directly edits a block file on the datanode and finds the changes reflected when reading through HDFS, revealing that checksums weren't enforced on reads.
quotes.key_insight: Strace can demystify even complex distributed systems like Hadoop by revealing the actual network calls and data flow at the system call level, turning opaque infrastructure into something you can observe, understand, and even tamper with.

quotes.quote: I often feel like the main (only?) thing I've learned about machine learning so far is how important it is to evaluate your models and make sure they're actually getting better.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recaps her experience at PyData NYC 2014, where she gave her first machine learning talk about the importance of evaluating models and tracking experimental results over time. Her talk introduced Stripe's open-source tool 'topmodel' for storing and visualizing training results. She also highlights other talks she enjoyed, including Sasha Laundy's talk on data auditing with csvkit and Dan Blanchard's SKLL framework for streamlined scikit-learn experimentation. The post captures the energy of attending a conference and learning from people working in different domains.
quotes.key_insight: The most valuable skill in machine learning isn't building models—it's systematically tracking and evaluating whether your experiments are actually making things better.

quotes.quote: I'd been hearing for a while that you can override arbitrary function calls in a program using an environment variable called LD_PRELOAD. But I didn't realize how easy it was!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans describes her experience discovering LD_PRELOAD at Hacker School with a fellow participant. She shows how surprisingly easy it is to override arbitrary function calls in dynamically linked programs by writing a tiny shared library and setting an environment variable. The post walks through a minimal example of replacing rand() to always return 42, then briefly explains the underlying mechanism of how dynamic linkers resolve function names. She also touches on the security implications, noting that LD_PRELOAD is useful for spying on program internals, and mentions defenses like static linking.
quotes.key_insight: LD_PRELOAD is a powerful and surprisingly easy-to-use mechanism for overriding function calls in dynamically linked programs, requiring only a few lines of C code.

quotes.quote: understanding the models I'm using helps me sleep a lot better at night
quotes.summary: Julia Evans walks through logistic regression from first principles, explaining how it fits a linear model and passes the output through a logistic function to produce probabilities. She demonstrates the technique by generating synthetic data that deliberately fits the model's assumptions, then fits a classifier using sklearn and evaluates it. Her key insight is that model evaluation should go beyond classification accuracy to check whether the predicted probabilities are calibrated—meaning a data point assigned 80% probability should actually be True about 80% of the time. She closes with open questions she cannot yet answer, modeling intellectual honesty about her own learning process.
quotes.key_insight: Model evaluation should include checking whether predicted probabilities are calibrated against actual class proportions, not just whether the binary classification is correct.

quotes.quote: I'm still working some of these things out, and if I've gotten anything terribly wrong I'd love to know about it
quotes.summary: Julia Evans walks through logistic regression from first principles, explaining how it fits a linear model and passes the output through a logistic function to produce probabilities. She demonstrates the technique by generating synthetic data that deliberately fits the model's assumptions, then fits a classifier using sklearn and evaluates it. Her key insight is that model evaluation should go beyond classification accuracy to check whether the predicted probabilities are calibrated—meaning a data point assigned 80% probability should actually be True about 80% of the time. She closes with open questions she cannot yet answer, modeling intellectual honesty about her own learning process.
quotes.key_insight: Model evaluation should include checking whether predicted probabilities are calibrated against actual class proportions, not just whether the binary classification is correct.

quotes.quote: I often feel like the only thing I've ever learned about machine learning is how important it is to be able to answer this question, and how hard it is.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans previews a talk she's preparing for PyData NYC about the practical challenges of evaluating machine learning models over time. She argues that the hardest part of machine learning isn't training models with scikit-learn, but answering 'how well does it work?' She references Cathy O'Neil's critiques of supposedly objective models to emphasize that measurement itself is fraught. The post introduces ROC curves and confusion matrices as evaluation tools, but pivots to her real interest: tracking model performance historically and designing systems that let you compare results across months and tools.
quotes.key_insight: The real challenge in machine learning isn't building models but designing systems to rigorously measure whether those models actually work, and tracking that over time.

quotes.quote: We should never trust a so-called 'objective mathematical model' when we can't even decide on a definition of success.

quotes.quote: EVERYONE IS DIFFERENT AND PEOPLE THINK LOTS OF DIFFERENT THINGS
quotes.summary: Evans shares results from a survey of 150+ women in technology, highlighting the wide range of experiences from deeply positive to deeply troubling. She emphasizes that women in tech are not a monolith — some feel valued and supported while others face harassment, ageism, and subtle career sabotage. The most popular reasons women cited for liking tech were enjoying the actual work and solving difficult problems. Evans presents extensive excerpts without editorializing, letting the diversity of voices speak for itself. She concludes with hope that workplaces can move toward making everyone feel valued, while acknowledging the heartbreaking gap between the best and worst experiences.
quotes.key_insight: Women in tech have enormously varied experiences, and acknowledging both the genuinely positive and genuinely terrible ones — without collapsing them into a single narrative — is essential to making progress.

quotes.quote: The worst thing about working remotely so far has just been feeling generally alienated.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her experience working remotely after 8 months, identifying feeling alienated as the biggest challenge. She connects this to self-determination theory, specifically the 'relatedness' component about feeling connected to others. She discovered that going 4 months without visiting the office was a mistake that led to negative feelings, and that in-person visits dramatically improved her wellbeing. She shares practical strategies like regular 1:1 meetings, frequent office visits, and daily routines to combat isolation.
quotes.key_insight: Remote work's biggest challenge isn't productivity or communication tools — it's maintaining the human connection and sense of relatedness that comes naturally from being physically present with colleagues.

quotes.quote: Turns out that if I don't see a group of people for way too long then it's easy to feel like nobody cares about me and everything I do is terrible, and it has all kinds of strange negative unforeseen consequences.

quotes.quote: I used to worry a lot about what my website looked like, and then I realized if I wrote interesting blog posts basically nobody cared!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans provides a quick-start guide for setting up a blog using Jekyll, Octopress, and GitHub Pages. She walks through the steps from installing Octopress to pushing a skeleton site to GitHub, emphasizing that the hardest part is getting Ruby working. The post's real message is that people overthink blog setup and design when the content is what actually matters. She admits the '5 minutes' claim is a lie but argues the low barrier to entry means there's no excuse not to start blogging.
quotes.key_insight: Stop worrying about how your blog looks and just start writing — interesting content matters far more than design.

quotes.quote: One of the most important things in database optimization is disk I/O. Reading more data than you absolutely need to read is really expensive, because seeking to a new location in a file takes a long time.
quotes.summary: This second part of Julia Evans' SQLite series explains how btrees minimize disk reads when querying a database. She demonstrates that SQLite organizes data into btree structures where each node has many children, keeping tree depth small and reducing the number of page reads needed. Using her 100,000-row test database, she shows that a btree of depth 3 requires only 3 page reads versus 16 for a binary tree. She also reveals that tables and indexes each have their own separate btrees, and walks through a concrete query showing binary searches happening within each page.
quotes.key_insight: Btrees minimize expensive disk seeks by keeping tree depth small through high branching factors, turning what would be 16 page reads in a binary tree into just 3 for a 100,000-row database.

quotes.quote: It takes way less CPU time to search through your data than it does to read the data into memory.

quotes.quote: btrees are organized so that each node has lots of children, to keep the depth small, and so that we won't have to read too many pages to find a row.

quotes.quote: My 100,000 row SQLite database has a btree with depth 3, so to fetch a node I only need to read 3 pages. If I'd used a binary tree I would have needed to do log(100000) / log(2) = 16 seeks!

quotes.quote: My favorite first thing to do with binary files is to cat them.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans and Kamal explore how SQLite stores data by creating a test database and examining its binary file format. They discover that SQLite databases are divided into fixed-size pages, with the page size encoded in the file header. By downloading the SQLite source code and adding a print statement to the btree page-reading function, they can trace exactly which pages are accessed during queries. The experiment reveals how B-tree indexing works in practice: nearby rows share page traversal paths, while distant rows diverge at different levels of the tree. The post demonstrates learning database internals through hands-on experimentation rather than just reading documentation.
quotes.key_insight: You can demystify database internals by adding a single print statement to the source code and watching which pages get accessed during queries.

quotes.quote: I've always understood indexes in a pretty vague way – I know that they're 'some kind of tree', which lets you access data in O(log n), and in particular that databases use something called a btree. I still do not really know what a btree is.

quotes.quote: I always imagine cool developer conferences like Strange Loop as being full of wizard developers who know everything about programming.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recounts her experience at Strange Loop 2014, where she gave a well-received talk on kernel hacking despite worrying it would be too entry-level. She reflects on her rapid growth from a nervous first lightning talk to speaking at a major conference. She highlights several standout talks, praises the conference's serious inclusivity efforts (implementing all 58 items on their inclusivity checklist), and critiques the static vs. dynamic typing debate for lacking empirical rigor.
quotes.key_insight: Making technical content more accessible rather than more advanced is almost always the right choice, because even expert audiences have surprising knowledge gaps.

quotes.quote: It turned out tons of people there didn't know a lot about systems programming / how kernels work, and my choice to make the talk accessible made a lot of people happy.

quotes.quote: So far I've given a bunch of talks and I've never regretted trying to make it easy to follow for as many people as possible.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recounts her experience at Strange Loop 2014, where she gave a well-received talk on kernel hacking despite worrying it would be too entry-level. She reflects on her rapid growth from a nervous first lightning talk to speaking at a major conference. She highlights several standout talks, praises the conference's serious inclusivity efforts (implementing all 58 items on their inclusivity checklist), and critiques the static vs. dynamic typing debate for lacking empirical rigor.

quotes.quote: kernel programming is not just for wizards, it can also be for me!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans argues that kernel programming is not an exclusive domain for wizards — anyone can learn it and benefit from the knowledge. She shares how she discovered during Hacker School that understanding Linux internals, particularly system calls, made her a better programmer even for high-level work. The post presents practical tools like strace, /proc, ftrace, and perf as reasons to care about the kernel, then offers six concrete strategies for learning: stracing programs, reading kernel source, writing kernel modules, building a toy OS, doing the Eudyptula challenge, and internships. She emphasizes that the kernel is fundamentally just C code you interact with through system calls, and demystifying it is both fun and practically useful.
quotes.key_insight: The Linux kernel is not magic reserved for wizards — it's approachable C code, and understanding it through tools like strace and simple kernel modules will make you a better programmer at any level.

quotes.quote: I used to think that executables were totally impenetrable. I'd compile a C program, and then that was it! I had a Magical Binary Executable that I could no longer read.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans demystifies binary executables by walking through the structure of a simple compiled C program on Linux. She explains the three key organizational concepts of ELF binaries: symbols (function names used for linking), sections (logical groupings like .text for code and .rodata for read-only data), and segments (runtime memory layout). Using tools like readelf, objdump, and nm, she shows how to inspect each layer, from raw bytes to disassembled instructions. The post emphasizes that executables are regular file formats that anyone can learn to read, not impenetrable magic.
quotes.key_insight: Binary executables aren't opaque magic — they're structured file formats with symbols, sections, and segments that anyone can inspect using standard Linux tools.

quotes.quote: I didn't care much about proper error handling or anything; I just wanted to get one webpage and declare victory :)
quotes.summary: Julia Evans describes her Hacker School project of writing a minimal TCP stack in Python using the scapy library, with the goal of fetching a webpage from Google. She walks through the TCP handshake, discovers that her kernel's existing TCP stack interferes by sending RST packets, and solves this with ARP spoofing to use a fake IP address. After getting it working, she finds that Python is too slow to send ACKs fast enough, causing Google's server to reset the connection. Despite only working 25% of the time, she considers the project more fun and instructive than writing a proper TCP stack in C.
quotes.key_insight: Building a deliberately impractical TCP stack in Python reveals how networking actually works — from kernel interference to timing constraints — in ways that reading specs or using proper tools never would.

quotes.quote: Once you're pairing with an asshole, you might as well stop. There's no point.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans revisits her enthusiasm for pair programming by collecting and categorizing reasons people find it difficult. After soliciting responses on Twitter, she received about 160 thoughtful replies describing challenges including exhaustion, anxiety, partner compatibility issues, and cognitive style mismatches. She organizes the feedback into clear categories: mental drain, typing anxiety, partner dynamics, thinking styles, dominance problems, and the preference for pair debugging over pair programming. The post concludes that pair programming is a useful tool but not universally beneficial, and understanding its difficulties leads to better pairing sessions.
quotes.key_insight: Pair programming is a powerful tool when used selectively, but it fails when people ignore the real challenges of exhaustion, anxiety, cognitive style differences, and power dynamics between partners.

quotes.quote: After all, if you've put in a ton of work to put a talk or workshop together, it's wonderful if other people can benefit from that as much as possible.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans advocates for open-sourcing talk and workshop materials so others can reuse and build on them. Inspired by Sumana Harihareshwara releasing a talk under CC-BY, she shares how her own pandas cookbook was reused for a workshop in the Philippines. She encourages others to freely reuse her talk materials and points to Software Carpentry's MIT-licensed lessons as another example of this approach.
quotes.key_insight: Open-sourcing talk and workshop materials multiplies their impact far beyond what any single speaker could achieve on their own.

quotes.quote: We talked about using systems programming tools (like strace) to debug your regular pedestrian code.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her appearance on the Ruby Rogues podcast, where she discussed systems programming tools like strace for debugging everyday code, building an OS in Rust, and the value of asking questions to learn. She expresses pleasant surprise at how the conversation ranged well beyond Ruby into topics like Rust, statistics, and strace. She notes the podcast referenced 13 of her blog posts, and reflects on the fun of cross-pollinating ideas across language communities.
quotes.key_insight: Systems programming tools and a willingness to ask naive questions are valuable across all programming communities, not just in low-level work.

quotes.quote: We are not fooled by small sample sizes and we ALREADY KNOW that we can't trust this information.
quotes.summary: Evans tackles the common question of how to determine appropriate sample sizes for experiments by walking through coin-flipping simulations. She demonstrates that small samples (10 trials) produce wildly unreliable results, while large samples (10,000 trials) cluster tightly around the true probability. She introduces hypothesis testing and p-values through an accessible mustard-preference thought experiment, using computer simulation rather than formulas. The post connects these empirical observations to the central limit theorem and normal distribution, explaining why historical statisticians could work without computers.
quotes.key_insight: Computer simulation makes hypothesis testing and sample size reasoning intuitive: instead of memorizing formulas, you can literally run the experiment thousands of times to see how likely your observed results would be under the null hypothesis.

quotes.quote: Your brother is arguing '7/10 seems like a lot! I like mustard! I totally believe this!'. You need to argue with him with MATH.

quotes.quote: it looks like a normal distribution, or 'bell curve'. That's not a coincidence! It's because of the central limit theorem! MATH IS AMAZING.
quotes.summary: Evans tackles the common question of how to determine appropriate sample sizes for experiments by walking through coin-flipping simulations. She demonstrates that small samples (10 trials) produce wildly unreliable results, while large samples (10,000 trials) cluster tightly around the true probability. She introduces hypothesis testing and p-values through an accessible mustard-preference thought experiment, using computer simulation rather than formulas. The post connects these empirical observations to the central limit theorem and normal distribution, explaining why historical statisticians could work without computers.
quotes.key_insight: Computer simulation makes hypothesis testing and sample size reasoning intuitive: instead of memorizing formulas, you can literally run the experiment thousands of times to see how likely your observed results would be under the null hypothesis.

quotes.quote: This also helps us understand how people could have calculated these probabilities back when we did not have computers but still needed to do statistics

quotes.quote: I wanted to work on things that seemed impossible to me, and writing an operating system seemed impossible.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her approach to Hacker School, a 12-week programming workshop, explaining how she deliberately ignored interesting-but-familiar topics to focus on things that felt impossible to her. Despite many appealing workshops in web development, machine learning, and new languages, she chose to pursue systems programming, networking, operating systems, and security—areas she knew nothing about. She emphasizes that saying no to interesting distractions and focusing on what scared her was key to her experience. She also highlights the value of learning from peers and creating what she wanted to exist.
quotes.key_insight: The most productive way to use unstructured learning time is to ignore interesting-but-familiar topics and focus relentlessly on things that feel impossible.

quotes.quote: I mostly did not go to these workshops. It turned out that I was interested in all those things, but more interested in learning: systems programming, networking, how to write an operating system.

She highlights the diverse range of topics covered at the conference, from EEG machines to dancing robots to hacking poetry. She credits Erty Seidel for doing the work of producing the talk recordings.
quotes.key_insight: !!Con featured an unusually diverse and playful range of technical talks, reflecting a community that values curiosity and joy in programming.

quotes.quote: I still don't think I could win a Kaggle competition. But I have a job where I do (among other things) machine learning! What gives?
quotes.summary: Julia Evans argues that real-world machine learning work is fundamentally different from Kaggle competitions. While Kaggle focuses on optimizing algorithms with clean data and pre-defined metrics, actual ML work involves understanding business problems, choosing appropriate metrics, collecting and cleaning messy data, deploying models to production, and monitoring ongoing performance. She shares her own experience of feeling demoralized by Kaggle leaderboards despite doing ML professionally, and encourages others not to equate Kaggle performance with ML competence.
quotes.key_insight: Real machine learning work is dominated by business understanding, data wrangling, and production engineering — the algorithm optimization that Kaggle tests is only a small slice of the job.

quotes.quote: I've spent time on projects where I didn't understand at all how the model was going to fit into business plans. If this is you, it doesn't matter how good your model is. At all.

quotes.quote: I've started trying to ask questions any time I don't understand something, instead of worrying about whether people will think I'm dumb for not knowing it. This is magical, because it means I can then learn those things!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans argues that asking questions, even about things you feel you 'should' already know, is one of the most powerful tools for learning. She shares how admitting she didn't understand what a Linux kernel does—despite using Linux for 10 years—led to deep learning and eventually giving talks on the topic. She highlights how even experts often don't know the answers to basic questions, citing Aphyr's discovery that RabbitMQ could lose 40% of data. The post advocates for saying 'I don't understand' openly, while being specific about what you do and don't know to avoid wasting anyone's time.
quotes.key_insight: Admitting what you don't know and asking questions openly—even about things you feel you should already know—is the fastest path to genuine understanding.

quotes.quote: I'd been using Linux for 10 years, and I didn't really understand at all what the basic responsibilities of the Linux kernel were.

quotes.quote: I didn't realize how much I depended on synchronous communication (talking face-to-face!) to do things until it was taken away from me.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her first three months working remotely for Stripe from the east coast while her team is in San Francisco. She honestly catalogs the difficulties, including timezone challenges, missing synchronous communication, and not knowing coworkers beyond IRC names. She also identifies genuine upsides like flexibility, working in the sun, and learning to optimize for happiness. The post frames remote work as a tradeoff worth making rather than an ideal arrangement, with practical strategies for coping.
quotes.key_insight: Remote work is sustainable when you treat happiness as a measurable variable, have other remote teammates, and reframe the tradeoffs as choices rather than losses.

quotes.quote: My happiness seems to be proportional to the amount of time I spend talking to people. This is something I can measure and optimize!

quotes.quote: It's a relief to know that I wasn't picked for gender, race, age, or anything like that.
quotes.summary: Evans examines whether conferences should adopt anonymous review processes for talk submissions, weighing the benefits against limitations. She confirms that anonymous review reduces bias, citing both speaker testimonials and academic research showing systemic gender bias in non-blind reviewing. However, she notes it won't automatically improve diversity since it can't fix low application rates from underrepresented groups. She presents a nuanced debate about whether anonymous review helps or hurts new speakers, with arguments on both sides. Her conclusion is that anonymous review is worthwhile but should be combined with active outreach, mentorship for new speakers, and strong calls for proposals.
quotes.key_insight: Anonymous review provably reduces bias and nepotism in conference talk selection, but it's not a silver bullet for diversity — it must be paired with active outreach and support for new speakers.

quotes.quote: I was honestly a bit scared by this – how were we going to make sure people were good at public speaking if we didn't even know who they were?! But it turned out my fears were totally unfounded.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans describes how anonymous talk review worked surprisingly well for !!Con, a conference she helped organize. Despite initial fears about not knowing speakers' identities, the process produced a diverse range of high-quality talks from both experienced and new speakers. The anonymization required significant effort from a dedicated team member who personally scrubbed all proposals. The result was a process where speakers felt confident they were judged on merit, and organizers couldn't give preferential treatment to famous speakers or friends.
quotes.key_insight: Anonymous talk review, while labor-intensive, produces fairer outcomes by forcing organizers to judge proposals purely on merit and giving speakers confidence that their selection was earned.

quotes.quote: Each of us assigned a proposal a letter grade (A-D), from 'I will fight for this talk', to 'I will fight for this talk to be rejected'.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans describes how anonymous talk review worked surprisingly well for !!Con, a conference she helped organize. Despite initial fears about not knowing speakers' identities, the process produced a diverse range of high-quality talks from both experienced and new speakers. The anonymization required significant effort from a dedicated team member who personally scrubbed all proposals. The result was a process where speakers felt confident they were judged on merit, and organizers couldn't give preferential treatment to famous speakers or friends.
quotes.key_insight: Anonymous talk review, while labor-intensive, produces fairer outcomes by forcing organizers to judge proposals purely on merit and giving speakers confidence that their selection was earned.

quotes.quote: None of this is to say that anonymous review is the best way to do talk review. But it worked very well for us.

quotes.quote: I knew how to navigate the filesystem from the command line, but not how to figure out how it works internally.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her hands-on exploration of HDFS internals by setting up an Amazon Elastic MapReduce cluster and using Spotify's snakebite Python library to understand how distributed file storage actually works. She walks through the process of discovering how files are split into blocks, how the namenode tracks block locations, and how data is read from data nodes. Along the way she uses strace to observe the actual network traffic, revealing the protocol buffer messages exchanged between client and cluster. The post captures the satisfaction of peeling back layers of abstraction to see raw Wikipedia text coming back from a distributed filesystem.
quotes.key_insight: You can demystify distributed systems by using tools like strace and readable client libraries to observe what actually happens on the wire when you read a file.

quotes.quote: strace only does system calls, and none of those things are system calls. So it can't tell you any of those things!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans introduces perf, a Linux profiling tool that goes beyond strace by revealing CPU-level performance details like instruction counts, cache misses, and branch predictions. She demonstrates three use cases: basic performance counters with perf stat, generating flame graphs to visualize where execution time is spent, and measuring L1 cache misses to confirm a hypothesis about why a program with bad memory access patterns was slow. The post builds on her previous exploration of why computers are fast, using perf to verify that a stride-based memory access pattern caused 60x more L1 cache misses. She references Brendan Gregg's perf resources as essential reading.
quotes.key_insight: perf transforms performance debugging from guesswork into empirical measurement by exposing CPU-level details like cache misses and instruction counts that are invisible to higher-level tools like strace.

quotes.quote: So today I paired with Sasha Laundy, and we ran Serious Investigations into how fast my computer can process a 1GB file.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores how fast her computer can process a 1GB file by writing progressively optimized C programs to sum all bytes. Starting with a simple implementation that runs in 0.6 seconds (after caching), she discovers that vector intrinsics (SIMD) can cut that to 0.25 seconds, while deliberately engineering cache misses makes it 40x slower. She uses perf to analyze where the remaining time is spent, finding roughly 32% on memory access and 68% on computation. The post demonstrates a hands-on, experimental approach to understanding hardware performance.
quotes.key_insight: By progressively optimizing a simple byte-summing program and deliberately breaking it, you can build concrete intuition about how CPU caches, SIMD instructions, and memory access patterns determine real-world performance.

quotes.quote: I realized I didn't need more information! What I had was enough, and I knew how to fix it.
quotes.summary: Evans recounts a debugging experience where she was stuck on a bug and frustrated by her inability to get more information through a debugger. After stepping away and thinking about it on her commute, she realized the error message she already had contained enough information to fix the problem. The post advocates for pausing to think before reaching for more tools or data.
quotes.key_insight: Before reaching for more tools or data, stop and think about whether you already have enough information to solve the problem.

quotes.quote: For a long time I had a vague feeling that I *should* be contributing to open source more, and that it was in some way a Good Thing. And I felt a little bit guilty about not doing it enough.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans pushes back against the vague guilt many developers feel about not contributing to open source. She uses Mel Chua's motivation framework of competence, autonomy, and relatedness to analyze why she does or doesn't contribute to specific projects. She argues that most open source projects fail the relatedness test—if you don't use it, don't want to learn about it, or don't believe in its mission, there's no reason to feel bad about not contributing. She shares examples of projects she has contributed to where motivation was genuine, and concludes that open source should only be pursued when it makes personal sense.
quotes.key_insight: Contributing to open source should be driven by genuine personal motivation—competence, autonomy, and relatedness—not by vague guilt or a sense of universal obligation.
