quotes.quote: I can look at the internal behavior of closed-source programs.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans argues that system calls provide a powerful debugging approach that doesn't require source code, a debugger, or print statements. By using tools like strace and ltrace, developers can observe exactly what any program is doing at the OS level — which files it opens, which commands it runs, and what data it writes. She walks through specific system calls (open, execve, write) and the /proc filesystem, showing practical examples with ssh, Chrome, and killall. Her core argument is that learning your operating system is a better investment than learning language-specific debuggers, since OS-level tools work across all languages and even on closed-source binaries.
quotes.key_insight: Learning to debug through OS-level tools like strace gives you universal, language-agnostic superpowers because system calls are the only interface between any program and the operating system.

quotes.quote: 1/3 of the talks were by women! It was wonderful.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recaps her first PyCon experience in 2014, where she gave a talk and was overwhelmed by the quality of the conference. She highlights roughly a dozen talks that stood out, spanning topics from computational biology and imposter syndrome to compiling Lisp to Python bytecode and analyzing rap lyrics with NLP. She notes that a third of talks were by women and emphasizes the practical, actionable nature of the best presentations. The post conveys genuine excitement about the Python community's inclusivity and intellectual energy.
quotes.key_insight: The best tech conferences combine intellectual rigor with genuine inclusivity, offering concrete calls to action rather than empty encouragement.

quotes.quote: How does someone become a senior developer? How do I know what I should be practicing? What qualities should I be looking for in mentors?
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores the question of how to grow from a developer into a senior engineer, prompted by conversations with friends and a Twitter discussion. She highlights John Allspaw's influential post 'On Being a Senior Engineer' as the best resource she found, emphasizing that seniority involves far more than technical skill. The post surfaces key non-technical qualities like empathy, seeking criticism, and raising up others. She also references @seriouspony's insight that deliberate practice matters more than volume, warning against the comfort of doing only what you already know.

quotes.quote: And it's not enough. Inevitably people will not ask because they're scared or they think it's their fault and then they'll get stuck.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans argues that simply telling beginners to 'ask if you have questions' at programming workshops is insufficient because people get stuck but won't speak up due to fear or self-blame. She shares specific tactics she uses as a workshop helper: actively circulating, repeatedly checking in with people, watching for confused faces, and responding to questions with genuine enthusiasm. The core message is that helpers need to be proactive rather than passive, going to the people rather than waiting to be flagged down.
quotes.key_insight: Effective teaching help requires proactive outreach and repeated check-ins, not passive availability, because people who are stuck rarely ask for help on their own.

quotes.quote: When I got a remote job, I thought it would be impossible to do pairing, or at the very least it would be a terrible experience.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her experience with remote pair programming after two weeks of working remotely. She was pleasantly surprised to find that remote pairing works nearly as well as in-person pairing, contrary to her expectations. She describes her toolset including tmate for terminal sharing, Google Hangouts/Skype for communication, and screensharing as an alternative. The main pain point is internet connectivity issues disrupting flow, but when the technology works, she finds remote pairing just as productive as in-person pairing.
quotes.key_insight: Remote pair programming can be surprisingly effective and feel as productive as in-person pairing, with internet reliability being the main bottleneck rather than the remote format itself.

quotes.quote: If you accidentally delete a file that a process still has open, you can recover it with cat /proc/$pid/fd/$file_descriptor.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her discovery that /proc can be used to recover accidentally deleted files that are still held open by a process, via /proc/$pid/fd/. She explores file descriptors, showing how they're symbolic links that reveal where processes direct their stdin, stdout, and stderr. She demonstrates practical uses like tracking which file a backup program is currently processing, and shows that even standard tools like ps get their information from /proc. The post ends with a list of related topics to investigate further.
quotes.key_insight: The /proc filesystem exposes kernel internals as ordinary files, enabling powerful capabilities like recovering deleted files and inspecting any process's state with basic shell commands.

quotes.quote: The reason I wrote an operating system in Rust wasn't so that I could have an operating system written in Rust.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explains why her toy Rust operating system will never be finished, and why that's perfectly fine. She distinguishes between programming projects meant to produce useful code versus projects meant for learning. Her OS project was firmly in the learning category, and by that measure it was a huge success—she learned about linkers, virtual memory, executables, system calls, and much more. She argues that the blog posts documenting what she learned are far more valuable than the code itself. She points to another developer's similar learning project as further evidence that the process and documentation matter more than the final product.
quotes.key_insight: A programming project's success should be measured against its actual goals—and for learning projects, the knowledge gained and documented matters far more than whether the code compiles or ships.

quotes.quote: My experience of writing a kernel that it was like jumping in puddles: it's a lot of fun, and there are a lot of mishaps.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her experience writing a tiny operating system in Rust, breaking it down into small incremental steps starting from copying an existing project called rustboot. She walks through turning the screen blue, writing I/O functions, handling keyboard interrupts, and implementing malloc, explaining the challenges at each stage. A key digression explains why you can't write a kernel in Python (because print ultimately calls the write system call, which is implemented in the kernel you're trying to write) but can in Rust using #[no_std] mode. The post emphasizes that even seemingly simple tasks like printing a string required days of learning about freestanding mode, Rust's type system, and rust-core.
quotes.key_insight: Writing an operating system becomes approachable when you break it into tiny steps and embrace the messiness of learning, starting from copying existing code and changing one color.

quotes.quote: You can always get help. Everyone takes responsibility for helping everyone else.
quotes.summary: Evans argues that Hacker School's secret to productivity is a culture where everyone takes responsibility for helping each other. She breaks down why help-seeking is efficient (saving hours of frustration), why it's different from handholding, and why creating a psychologically safe environment for questions is essential. She challenges the notion that self-directed learners don't need help, arguing that support turns autonomous people into superheroes. The post reframes helping as core work rather than a distraction, and proposes that answering others' questions should replace typical procrastination habits.
quotes.key_insight: A culture where everyone takes responsibility for helping others doesn't create dependency — it turns already-capable people into superheroes by removing the friction that makes learning slow and painful.

quotes.quote: It's official. I have a love affair with strace.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans demonstrates how strace can solve shared library version conflicts by tracing which files a program actually opens. She was trying to install IRuby notebook but kept getting the wrong version of libzmq loaded despite upgrading it. By using strace to trace system calls and grepping for the library name, she discovered two different versions of libzmq were being loaded from different paths. Removing the outdated version fixed the problem.
quotes.key_insight: When shared library conflicts defy normal debugging, strace reveals exactly which files a program loads at runtime, cutting through all the confusion about library paths and versions.

quotes.quote: Two humans sit down in front of the same computer, with one keyboard, and work on a Thing together.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans advocates for pair programming based on her experience at Hacker School and her new job at Stripe. She defines it simply as two people working at one computer together, and frames it as an extended version of something developers already do naturally—asking someone to look at code with them. She lists practical benefits including timeboxing, knowledge sharing, and making boring tasks enjoyable, while acknowledging that misaligned goals can cause sessions to go badly. Her core message is that even occasional, casual pairing produces better code and more learning.
quotes.key_insight: Pair programming doesn't require a formal methodology or full-time commitment—even casual, timeboxed sessions produce better code, more learning, and more fun.

quotes.quote: There were so many excellent suggestions that I couldn't not share!
quotes.summary: Evans shares community suggestions for practical uses of strace, collected after her previous post on using strace to avoid reading Ruby code. The suggestions range from debugging running Ruby processes with strace/gdb/lsof, to finding shared library loading paths, analyzing git performance with strace -c, and using related tools like ltrace and system-wide syscall tracing. She highlights several external resources including blog posts and slides that demonstrate strace as a versatile debugging tool.
quotes.key_insight: strace is just the starting point of a rich ecosystem of tracing tools — ltrace, system-wide syscall tracing, and dtruss — that together form a powerful debugging toolkit for understanding what programs are actually doing.

quotes.quote: He remarks that some of the things are like open-heart surgery – you can go into a running Ruby process and execute code using gdb, but you might kill the process.

quotes.quote: The normal way I'd think about doing this is by, well, reading the code. But that takes time!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans describes using strace to quickly figure out what SSH command an internal Ruby tool was running, instead of reading through the Ruby source code. By running strace with the -f flag to follow child processes and filtering for execve system calls, she and a colleague were able to see the exact SSH command being executed. The post advocates for using system-level debugging tools as a faster alternative to reading unfamiliar code, and frames it as an example of how understanding system calls has practical everyday value.
quotes.key_insight: System-level tools like strace can answer questions about program behavior faster than reading source code, making them practical debugging shortcuts for everyday programming.

quotes.quote: This is super fun because what I'd usually do is go read the code to try and figure out what it's doing, and reading code is hard! strace is easy!

quotes.quote: Hey! If you can look at the system calls with strace and the library calls with ltrace, can you spy on people's ssh passwords?!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans demonstrates how strace can be used to spy on SSH passwords by attaching to the sshd process and reading system call arguments. She walks through the process of finding the child sshd process, attaching strace to it, and observing the password appear in plaintext in the read() system call output. The key insight is that strace doesn't just show which system calls are running—it shows their arguments, including sensitive data like passwords. She notes this requires root access so it's not a vulnerability per se, but it illustrates how thoroughly compromised a machine becomes once root is obtained.
quotes.key_insight: strace exposes not just system call names but their full arguments, meaning any process handling sensitive data like passwords can be trivially surveilled by anyone with root access.

quotes.quote: The reason this is possible is that strace doesn't just tell you which system calls a given program is running. It also tells you what the arguments are!

quotes.quote: The attitude was 'Oh, is something not working? How delightful! A learning opportunity!'.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her three-month experience at Hacker School (now Recurse Center), describing it as one of the best experiences of her life. She highlights the welcoming, positive atmosphere where nobody is told they can't do something. The environment's energy was so strong that her worry about lacking motivation proved laughably wrong. She credits the carefully chosen residents, skilled facilitators, and curated admissions process for creating a community of curious, friendly people. The piece serves as both a personal reflection and an enthusiastic recommendation for others to apply.
quotes.key_insight: The best learning environments are built not from curriculum or credentials but from a deliberately curated culture of curiosity, positivity, and mutual respect.

quotes.quote: I'm trying to read more blogs about things I don't know much about yet, to expandify my mind.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares a curated list of blogs she enjoys reading, spanning topics from distributed systems and Postgres to game development and finance. She emphasizes her deliberate strategy of reading outside her comfort zone to expand her knowledge. The list highlights mostly women and underrepresented voices in tech, covering both technical and non-technical subjects. Each recommendation comes with specific post suggestions and genuine enthusiasm for what she's learned from each writer.
quotes.key_insight: Deliberately reading blogs outside your expertise — even when you don't fully understand them — is a powerful way to expand your thinking and discover unexpected connections.

quotes.quote: Debugging C programs used to scare me a lot.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares a beginner-friendly introduction to GDB, the C debugger, motivated by her experience debugging her own operating system. She walks through three fundamental GDB operations: setting breakpoints, viewing disassembled code, and examining memory contents. The post uses a minimal C program to demonstrate each concept with concrete examples. She emphasizes that just knowing breakpoints and memory examination got her surprisingly far in real debugging work.
quotes.key_insight: You don't need to master GDB to get real value from it - just learning breakpoints and memory examination can carry you surprisingly far in debugging C programs.

quotes.quote: The main thing I used gdb for when I was debugging my kernel was to examine regions of memory to make sure they were what I thought they were.

quotes.quote: I have been known to answer questions like 'do you know statistics?' with 'no, I don't know anything about statistics', followed by an eventual admission of 'well except that I have a math degree'.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her struggle with underselling herself in technical interviews and offers practical strategies for sounding more confident. She identifies a common pattern of defaulting to 'I don't know anything' when she actually has substantial knowledge. Her three strategies are: stating factual experience instead of hedging, treating interview questions as genuine knowledge-sharing opportunities, and leading with what you do know rather than what you don't. The post is a concise, personal reflection on interview communication.
quotes.key_insight: Replacing vague self-deprecation with factual statements about your experience is a simple but powerful way to communicate confidence in interviews.

quotes.quote: I struggle with underselling myself, and I forget sometimes that 'I don't have a PhD in this' isn't the same as 'I don't know anything'.

quotes.quote: Pretend that they don't know the answer, but that they're really really smart.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her struggle with underselling herself in technical interviews and offers practical strategies for sounding more confident. She identifies a common pattern of defaulting to 'I don't know anything' when she actually has substantial knowledge. Her three strategies are: stating factual experience instead of hedging, treating interview questions as genuine knowledge-sharing opportunities, and leading with what you do know rather than what you don't. The post is a concise, personal reflection on interview communication.
quotes.key_insight: Replacing vague self-deprecation with factual statements about your experience is a simple but powerful way to communicate confidence in interviews.

quotes.quote: She pointed out that people who write imperative programs spend a lot of time learning how to read for loops, and learning how to read map is similar.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recaps her experience attending and speaking at CUSEC, an undergraduate software engineering conference. She highlights favorite talks on contributing to Firefox, functional programming, and probabilistic data structures. She describes organizing CUSECLadies, an informal meetup connecting women students with experienced women in tech. She also shares her excitement about giving her 'You can be a kernel hacker!' talk and a useful presentation technique of graying out irrelevant code.
quotes.key_insight: Making technical topics less intimidating — whether through talk design, community building, or simple presentation tricks like graying out irrelevant code — is what turns curiosity into confidence.

quotes.quote: A whole bunch of delightful people came up to me after and told me that they now feel like they know what a kernel is and how to get started with kernel programming!

quotes.quote: In the last month I've done what feels like 100 interviews. It's easier than I thought it would be.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her experience from 15-30 technical interviews during a job search, breaking down the types of questions she encountered. She categorizes them into background questions and technical questions, offering practical advice on how to approach each. She notes that the industry seems to be moving away from brainteaser questions toward more practical coding and system design questions. She finds that having unusual technologies on her resume sparks interesting conversations, and that interview preparation doubles as useful self-reflection.
quotes.key_insight: Technical interviews are less intimidating than expected, and the industry is shifting toward practical, project-based questions that reward genuine curiosity and diverse experience over rote problem-solving tricks.

quotes.quote: The first order of business when preparing a talk is to convince yourself that public speaking is a good idea and better than hiding under the bed.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans makes the case that more people should give conference talks, drawing on her early experiences speaking at PyCon Canada, PyData NYC, and local meetups. She addresses the fear of public speaking by referencing Hilary Mason's argument that talks are actually efficient for introverts since you introduce yourself to everyone at once. She counters perfectionism by suggesting you compare yourself to mediocre talks rather than the best ones. The core preparation advice, drawn from the Serious Pony blog, is that audiences care about becoming more awesome themselves, not about your polish or presentation skills.
quotes.key_insight: The secret to good talks isn't polish or presentation skills — it's giving your audience something they can actually use to become better at what they do.

quotes.quote: If you're an introvert, public speaking is an excellent idea because introducing yourself is a lot of work. If you give a talk, you only have to introduce yourself to everyone once.

quotes.quote: If you don't *need* to understand the kernel for your work, why would you try?
quotes.summary: Julia Evans pushes back against gatekeeping advice she received about learning kernel programming and offers four accessible paths for getting started. She was told that without a professional need, there's no reason to learn about the kernel, and that subscribing to LKML was the way in. Instead, she proposes writing your own OS (starting from existing projects), writing kernel modules, doing a Linux kernel internship through outreach programs, and reading kernel source code with cross-referencing tools. Each path is presented as genuinely fun and achievable without deep expertise.
quotes.key_insight: The Linux kernel isn't scary if you ignore the gatekeepers and find your own fun, incremental path in — whether that's turning a screen purple, writing an 18-line module, or reading chmod's source code.

quotes.quote: I now totally feel like I could write a production OS if I were given some hardware, 20 years and an army of volunteers.
quotes.summary: Evans describes the kernel she wrote at Hacker School by listing everything it can't do, using that as a framework for explaining what kernels actually handle. Her kernel can only print to the screen, understand keyboard inputs, and almost run programs. By enumerating missing capabilities like filesystem support, networking, scheduling, and memory protection, she makes the scope of kernel work concrete and approachable. She's preparing a conference talk arguing that kernel programming is something normal humans can do, and frames the learning process as picking a specific subsystem and diving in.
quotes.key_insight: Building a minimal kernel that can barely do anything is a powerful way to demystify what production kernels actually handle, making the whole domain feel approachable.

quotes.quote: The idea is that once you know what a kernel does, you can pick a Thing and a Kernel, and then dive into it.

quotes.quote: Asking a lot of questions shows that you value yourself and that you're careful when making decisions. It's a good thing.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her comprehensive list of questions to ask when interviewing at companies, organized into categories like engineering practices, management style, quality of life, and culture. She advocates spending 20 minutes before each interview selecting relevant questions from the list. The post emphasizes that asking many questions signals self-respect and careful decision-making. She highlights the importance of 'statistical' questions that reveal actual company behavior rather than stated policies, like asking what time people leave rather than whether there are set hours.
quotes.key_insight: The best interview questions probe actual company behavior through statistical and situational evidence rather than accepting stated policies at face value.

quotes.quote: A company may say that they 'don't have hours', but if everyone leaves at 9pm that's not a good sign.

quotes.quote: One of the hardest and most memorable coding exercises that I've done was to spend 30 minutes writing code that I really thought was good, and then delete it.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares a collection of unconventional coding strategies, presented as a series of tweets and brief reflections. The strategies emphasize deliberate practice techniques like writing code and then deleting it, writing the README before the code, and coding without internet access. She also includes contributions from Erik Åsheim, who adds strategies around self-conscious interrogation of decisions and physical exercise as a coding tool. The post frames programming improvement as something that comes from intentional constraints and varied approaches rather than just writing more code.
quotes.key_insight: Imposing deliberate constraints and varied exercises on your coding practice builds skills more effectively than just writing code the usual way.

She also includes contributions from Erik Åsheim, who adds strategies around self-conscious interrogation of decisions and physical exercise as a coding tool. The post frames programming improvement as something that comes from intentional constraints and varied approaches rather than just writing more code.
quotes.key_insight: Imposing deliberate constraints and varied exercises on your coding practice builds skills more effectively than just writing code the usual way.

quotes.quote: This person said 'my friends totally think it is!', and I verified with my friends, who totally said it wasn't. Not the best grounds for a discussion.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans conducted an informal survey of about 2,300 people to investigate whether 'guys' is perceived as a gender-neutral term, after a disagreement where different friend groups had opposing views. She found a significant gender split in perception: about 50% of men considered terms like 'Java guys' gender neutral, while only 25% of women did. She acknowledges the survey's limitations including selection bias and framing issues, but considers it a successful way to generate productive, non-heated discussion about language use.
quotes.key_insight: Whether 'guys' feels gender-neutral depends heavily on who you ask—men are roughly twice as likely as women to perceive it as neutral, revealing a blind spot that a simple survey can surface.

quotes.quote: What strace does is capture every single system call that gets called when executing a program.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explores how the killall command works internally by tracing its system calls with strace. She discovers that killall performs an exhaustive search through every PID in /proc, reading each process's stat file to check its name against the target. When it finds a match, it sends the appropriate signal (SIGTERM by default, SIGKILL with -9). The post also introduces ltrace for seeing library-level calls like strcmp, and mentions attaching strace to running processes.
quotes.key_insight: Using strace to trace system calls reveals that killall performs a brute-force search through every process in /proc, showing how simple tools can teach you about Linux internals.

quotes.quote: System calls are the interface between userspace programs and the kernel, so looking at the output from strace is a fun way to understand how Linux works, and what's really involved in running a program.

quotes.quote: A lot of them are taken from talks I've given, but I also want to give some new examples, like how to deal with timestamps, what is a pivot table and why would you ever want one?
quotes.summary: Julia Evans announces her pandas-cookbook, a GitHub repository of worked examples for learning the Python pandas library. The cookbook grew out of slides from her talks that people found helpful but which kept going out of date. Each chapter uses real datasets to teach pandas concepts progressively, from reading CSVs to cleaning messy data and parsing timestamps. The approach emphasizes learning by doing with real-world data rather than abstract documentation.
quotes.key_insight: The best way to learn a data tool like pandas is through worked examples on real datasets that answer concrete questions, not through abstract documentation.

quotes.quote: It's along the same lines as the pandas talks I've given – take a real dataset or three, play around with it, and learn how to use pandas along the way.

quotes.quote: I WILL WRITE 4000 BLOG POSTS TOO.
quotes.summary: Julia reflects on her last day at Hacker School (now Recurse Center), expressing sadness about leaving and gratitude for the community. She shares a conversation with Sumana Harihareswara about continuing to blog after Hacker School ends. The exchange captures the 'never graduate' ethos — that learning and creating don't stop when a program ends. She resolves to keep blogging, coding, and learning beyond the structured environment.
quotes.key_insight: The end of a structured learning program isn't the end of learning — the 'never graduate' mindset turns a bittersweet goodbye into a commitment to lifelong curiosity.

quotes.quote: Oh, but what will I write about when Hacker School is over? I will have nothing to say!

quotes.quote: I wanted to be able to run executables because THAT WOULD BE COOL.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her progress writing a kernel in Rust, specifically getting to the point where she can parse ELF headers and execute simple programs. After receiving help from Graydon Hoare (Rust's creator), she got a minimal 897-byte test program to work with instead of an 800k Hello World. She walks through the steps needed to load and run an ELF executable: reading headers, finding segments, copying them into memory, and jumping to the entry point. The post shows her actual Rust code for parsing ELF headers and executing programs, noting the heavy use of unsafe code.
quotes.key_insight: Running executables from a hobby kernel boils down to parsing ELF headers and jumping to the right memory address, and working with a minimal test program makes each step understandable.

quotes.quote: This actually ended up being really easy to debug! "Really easy" as in "it took all day, but I did not feel like hiding under the table at any point".
quotes.summary: Julia describes a day spent debugging her OS's interrupt descriptor table by switching the implementation from assembly to Rust. She found two bugs—an operator precedence error and a wrong function name—by using GDB to compare memory contents between working and non-working code. Despite taking all day, the debugging felt manageable thanks to GDB's memory examination tools. The fix lets her set up interrupt handlers in Rust, moving her closer to being able to run programs on her OS.
quotes.key_insight: GDB's ability to examine and compare raw memory contents makes debugging low-level OS code systematic rather than terrifying, even when bugs take all day to find.

quotes.quote: SURPRISE MY CODE IS NOT WORKING BECAUSE SOMETHING IS ERASING IT.
quotes.summary: Julia recounts a frustrating day debugging her hobby OS project, where a mysterious bug kept zeroing out her array contents at runtime. After upgrading Rust and rust-core (which broke her build), she worked through the compilation issues only to hit the same recurring mystery bug. With help from Allison Kaptur and GDB debugging, she discovered that half her binary was being overwritten with zeros at runtime. The root cause turned out to be embarrassingly simple: her bootloader was only loading 12KB of the binary, so everything beyond that was missing.
quotes.key_insight: The most baffling bugs often have the most mundane root causes — in this case, days of mysterious runtime corruption traced back to a hardcoded size constant in a bootloader.

quotes.quote: I've discovered that this "set stuff up so that a program can run" business is called loading, and what I'm doing is writing a loader. Sweet.
quotes.summary: Julia documents her attempt to figure out how to run a simple ELF executable inside her hobby operating system (called 'puddle'). She breaks down the problem into concrete steps: parsing ELF headers, implementing system call handlers, finding the entry point, and jumping to it. She explicitly lists what she's skipping for now (virtual memory, security, scheduling) and what questions remain unanswered, particularly around position-independent code and address resolution. The post captures the messy, exploratory middle of a learning project where the path forward is only partially clear.
quotes.key_insight: Breaking an intimidating systems problem into a checklist of concrete steps and explicit unknowns transforms it from overwhelming to tractable.

quotes.quote: I've fixed it, but I don't understand *why* it's fixed, and I am having no luck.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents a frustrating day spent debugging a linker problem in her OS project. She and a pair programming partner discovered that ELF files only work when sections are ordered in a specific way (.text .rodata .data .bss), but she can't figure out why. Despite fixing the issue, the lack of understanding is clearly bothering her. She posted a StackOverflow question that went unanswered and began writing a tutorial on binary formats as a consolation project.
quotes.key_insight: Fixing a bug without understanding why it's fixed is deeply unsatisfying, and linker internals are one of those areas where even community resources fall short.

quotes.quote: Linkers don't care about your functions and variables! They're just bytes.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her deep dive into how linkers work, motivated by a linker bug in her kernel project. She summarizes the first 11 parts of Ian Lance Taylor's 20-part series on linkers, covering core concepts like relocations, ELF sections and segments, shared libraries, position-independent code, thread local storage, and symbol versioning. Throughout, she uses hands-on exploration with tools like objdump and readelf to make abstract concepts concrete. The post serves as both personal reference notes and an accessible introduction to linker internals.
quotes.key_insight: Linkers are surprisingly complex systems that handle relocations, position independence, thread-local storage, and symbol versioning — but at their core they're just combining bytes and filling in addresses.

quotes.quote: A benchmark you could compare a linker to is cat, because it's just combining the bytes together, and making some replacements along the way.

quotes.quote: Those `.a` files? Those are just collections of `.o` object files, and they're called "archives"!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents 12 things she learned about linkers from reading an 11-part series on LWN. The post covers ELF file structure, including segments vs sections, symbol types and visibility, relocation tables, and shared library linking. She explains practical tools like objdump, readelf, and nm for inspecting object files. The post ends with a curiosity about why symbol name mangling is used for polymorphism instead of ELF symbol versions.
quotes.key_insight: Linkers are far more complex than they appear, handling symbol resolution, memory optimization for shared libraries, threading awareness, and relocation — all while trying to run as fast as cat.

quotes.quote: Linkers actually have to do fairly complicated stuff to allow the code in a shared library to be shared between different programs and save memory.

quotes.quote: My major achievement for today is writing the following five lines of code and having them do the wrong thing.
quotes.summary: Julia describes writing a deliberately broken malloc implementation in Rust as part of her OS project at Hacker School. Her first version always returns the same memory address, causing every variable to share the same value. She then shows the working version that properly increments a base pointer. She notes that writing malloc is easy if you never need to free memory, and the hardest part was actually getting the Rust type signatures right.
quotes.key_insight: Writing a basic malloc is surprisingly simple — the real complexity lives in freeing memory and satisfying the type system, not in allocation itself.

quotes.quote: After 6 days, I have problems that I can't understand at all
quotes.summary: Evans documents a baffling array indexing bug in her Rust-based OS kernel where NUMS[2] and NUMS[keycode] produce different results even when keycode provably equals 2. The bug only manifests when the conditional checks for multiple keycodes, suggesting compiler optimization interactions. She methodically demonstrates the contradiction through output analysis and rules out obvious explanations. The fix turned out to be switching from raw binary linking to ELF format with objcopy, though she doesn't fully understand why that resolved it.
quotes.key_insight: When writing OS-level code, the most bewildering bugs often stem not from logic errors but from toolchain issues like linking format, where the abstraction layers between your code and the hardware silently break assumptions.

quotes.quote: Right now I'm working towards being able to: 1. press keys on my keyboard 2. having the OS not crash 3. and have the key I pressed be echoed back
quotes.summary: Julia documents her multi-day struggle to get keyboard interrupts working in her hobby operating system written in Rust. She walks through 16 steps of debugging, from creating descriptor tables to remapping the PIC, each step revealing a new way the OS could crash. The post captures the grueling iterative nature of OS development, where each fix reveals the next obscure problem. After five days, she achieved the milestone of pressing a key without crashing, though initially only for the first keypress.
quotes.key_insight: OS development is a chain of obscure, interdependent low-level details where each fix reveals the next crash, making every small milestone — like pressing a key without crashing — a genuine triumph.

quotes.quote: My OS literally can't allocate memory, and there's no standard way to print (I have to write to the VGA buffer manually).
quotes.summary: Julia Evans describes the experience of writing a kernel in Rust without access to malloc, standard libraries, or basic OS facilities. She catalogs what's unavailable—memory allocation, printing, sleep, files, stdin—and finds it surprisingly manageable. The constraint forces all memory to be compile-time or stack-allocated, which gives her new appreciation for why the Linux kernel uses so many global variables. She shares VGA buffer code that writes characters directly to screen memory.
quotes.key_insight: Programming without malloc on bare metal is surprisingly tractable and reveals why kernel code relies on global variables—when you only need one of something, dynamic allocation is pointless overhead.

quotes.quote: I found understanding Rust types really confusing, so I wrote up a small tutorial for myself in an attempt to understand some of them.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans wrote a beginner-friendly tutorial on Rust's type system to help herself understand it. She covers primitive types, vectors, strings, functions, closures, and raw pointers with practical examples. The post acknowledges that Rust's type system is confusing and that the official documentation lacks sufficient examples. She provides workarounds for common frustrations like the lack of a REPL and difficulty discovering variable types. The tutorial reflects early Rust (pre-1.0) when the language was rapidly changing, with syntax like owned pointers (~) and managed pointers (@) that have since been removed.
quotes.key_insight: Writing a tutorial for yourself, complete with practical examples and workarounds, is an effective way to learn a confusing type system when the official documentation falls short.

quotes.quote: As far as I understand it, a function call is just jumping to an address in memory and putting some stuff on the stack and in registers, and the code doesn't care at all if that address in memory comes from Rust or C or assembly or what.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explains the mechanics of calling Rust functions from assembly and vice versa, as part of her project building a kernel in Rust. She walks through the key concepts: calling conventions, object files, symbols, and linkers. The post demonstrates how the `extern` keyword works in both assembly (NASM) and Rust to declare functions defined elsewhere. She shows how a linker script combines the object files into a single binary by matching symbol tables. The post demystifies linker errors by explaining they simply mean a symbol wasn't found in any linked object file, and shows how to inspect symbol tables with `objdump`.
quotes.key_insight: Cross-language function calls between Rust and assembly are demystified once you understand that linkers simply match symbol names across object files, regardless of the source language.

quotes.quote: This is like a function declaration in C, except without the types.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans explains the mechanics of calling Rust functions from assembly and vice versa, as part of her project building a kernel in Rust. She walks through the key concepts: calling conventions, object files, symbols, and linkers. The post demonstrates how the `extern` keyword works in both assembly (NASM) and Rust to declare functions defined elsewhere. She shows how a linker script combines the object files into a single binary by matching symbol tables. The post demystifies linker errors by explaining they simply mean a symbol wasn't found in any linked object file, and shows how to inspect symbol tables with `objdump`.
quotes.key_insight: Cross-language function calls between Rust and assembly are demystified once you understand that linkers simply match symbol names across object files, regardless of the source language.

quotes.quote: I'm not sure how well it translates to video, since it was a pretty interactive tutorial and I spent a lot of the time running around in the audience answering questions.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares that videos from PyData NYC 2013 are now available, including her own IPython Notebook and pandas tutorial. She highlights several talks she enjoyed, particularly one on probabilistic data structures and Bloom filters, and another on Python at Datadog that demonstrated Cython's code optimization visualization. The post serves as a brief conference recap with links to resources and recommendations for talks worth watching.
quotes.key_insight: Conferences are valuable for discovering unexpected tools and techniques — like Cython's optimization visualization — that you wouldn't encounter in your normal workflow.

quotes.quote: So far I've made some minor changes – it now turns the screen *green* and hangs.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her experience starting to write a kernel in Rust using the rustboot project and rust-core library. After being encouraged by Lindsey Kuper to try Rust for her OS project, she progressed from turning the screen green to writing functions that print characters to the VGA buffer. She shares the actual code for writing to VGA memory and iterating over string bytes, along with the cryptic import requirements needed to make basic features work in freestanding Rust. The post highlights both the satisfaction of low-level programming without an OS and the challenges of working with an early-stage systems language.
quotes.key_insight: Writing a kernel in Rust is possible with minimal scaffolding, but freestanding Rust development requires navigating cryptic import dependencies and relying heavily on community support to get even basic features working.

quotes.quote: The question: If I were an OS, what would I need to do to run 'Hello, world?'
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her attempt to understand what happens at the OS level when running a simple 'Hello, world' program. She posted her current understanding as a GitHub gist and asked Twitter for corrections and additions, receiving many helpful comments. The post walks through the key steps: loading code and data segments into memory, setting up the heap and stack, and handling system calls. She simplifies the program by replacing printf with a direct write() call and uses strace to observe the actual system calls made. Throughout, she's honest about gaps in her understanding, particularly around virtual memory and certain system calls like arch_prctl.
quotes.key_insight: Even the simplest program involves a complex chain of OS operations — loading segments, setting up memory, handling system calls — and the best way to learn this is to strip away abstractions and examine what actually happens.

quotes.quote: I discovered that it was impossible to explain my TCP fun project because it was, er, mostly not working.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans wraps up her TCP stack project at Hacker School by writing a minimal curl implementation on top of it. The tool uses ARP spoofing to bypass the kernel's built-in TCP stack, listening on a fake IP address to avoid the kernel resetting connections. She candidly documents that the implementation is finicky and unreliable, often requiring multiple runs to succeed. The post catalogs both what works (connecting, sending packets, reassembling replies) and what doesn't (multi-packet sends, retransmission, server functionality).
quotes.key_insight: Building a TCP stack from scratch reveals how much invisible complexity the kernel handles, even when the result barely works.

quotes.quote: One of my as-yet-unrealized goals for Hacker School is to write an operating system, at least a tiny one.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her first steps toward writing a tiny operating system during Hacker School. She decides to target x86 so she can run it on real hardware, finds the MikeOS tutorial as a starting resource, and gets a minimal OS running in an emulator. The OS can print characters to the screen and change the background color using BIOS interrupt handlers. It's a brief, enthusiastic update documenting the very beginning of an OS project.
quotes.key_insight: Getting started with OS development is more accessible than it seems — a few tools and a good tutorial can get you from zero to printing characters on screen in a day.

quotes.quote: As always when starting a new project I have been consulting approximately a million resources to get started.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares a curated collection of resources for making music with ClojureScript, compiled during her Hacker School project to practice change ringing. The post covers three main areas: getting started with ClojureScript, using the Web Audio API for sound synthesis in the browser, and working with sound samples from freesound.org. Rather than a tutorial, it's a practical reference list born from the frustration of repeatedly searching for the same resources. The post reflects her learning-in-public approach during her Hacker School batch.
quotes.key_insight: When learning a new technology, collecting and sharing the scattered resources you find is itself a valuable contribution.

quotes.quote: Then a sound plays on my computer! Like a crow, or a cowbell.
quotes.summary: Julia describes building a 'Magical Orchestra' project in Clojure with collaborator Lyndsey during Hacker School. The web app lets people visit a URL on their phones and trigger sounds (like a crow or cowbell) that play on the host computer. They demonstrated it at presentations by plugging into a speaker and letting everyone make noise together. It's a short, playful post celebrating a fun collaborative hack.
quotes.key_insight: Some of the most delightful programming projects are simple, playful tools that let people interact together in unexpected ways.

quotes.quote: So at presentations we plugged my computer into a speaker and let everyone make noise. It was delightful.

quotes.quote: My favorite thing about logic programming is that all the functions end with 'o'.
quotes.summary: Julia describes pairing with Will Byrd to implement binary search trees using core.logic in Clojure. The key revelation is that logic programming lets you run functions 'backwards' — instead of just checking if a tree contains a value, you can ask for trees that contain a given value. She shows the code for a containso function and demonstrates both forward and backward queries, generating 10 different binary trees that all contain the number 3.
quotes.key_insight: Logic programming's power lies in running programs backwards — instead of just verifying properties, you can generate all structures that satisfy a given constraint.

quotes.quote: But the really fun part is that you can run it "backwards" and ask for 10 trees that contain 3.

quotes.quote: On the box it says that you need to be "comfortable with" Clojure, the basics of music theory, and the Supercollider audio synthesis environment. I do not know any of these things and I successfully made sounds by copying and changing examples.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her first day using Overtone, a Clojure library for generating music through code. She successfully made sounds by copying examples despite not knowing Clojure, music theory, or Supercollider. She encountered her first Clojure bug involving lazy evaluation, where map wouldn't execute side effects inside a recursive function. The fix was using doseq instead of map for side-effecty operations. She also shares her growing comfort with Emacs and paredit for Clojure development.
quotes.key_insight: You don't need to meet all the stated prerequisites to start making things — copying examples and hitting real bugs is a valid and effective way to learn.

quotes.quote: I no longer know how to write if statements without looking it up! Very Exciting Times!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her first day using Overtone, a Clojure library for generating music through code. She successfully made sounds by copying examples despite not knowing Clojure, music theory, or Supercollider. She encountered her first Clojure bug involving lazy evaluation, where map wouldn't execute side effects inside a recursive function. The fix was using doseq instead of map for side-effecty operations. She also shares her growing comfort with Emacs and paredit for Clojure development.
quotes.key_insight: You don't need to meet all the stated prerequisites to start making things — copying examples and hitting real bugs is a valid and effective way to learn.

quotes.quote: I've been watching all the Lisp wizards with their emacs attached to magical running Lisp processes where they can change and reload the code in real time. And I am officially jealous.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans describes her decision to try Emacs after watching Lisp programmers at Hacker School use it with live-reloading capabilities. She's motivated by wanting to use Overtone for music generation in Clojure and was inspired by a talk on logic programming and automatic quine generation. She plans to explore Clojure's core.logic library. She notes she'll use evil mode to keep her vim keybindings.
quotes.key_insight: Seeing the live-coding workflow of Lisp programmers using Emacs can be compelling enough to make even a committed vim user switch editors.

quotes.quote: Definitely using evil mode, though, because I haven't spent years practicing vim keybindings for nothing.

quotes.quote: You tell it the sequence of git commands you've ever executed. It gives you back pretty pictures.
quotes.summary: Julia built a web application that visualizes Git workflows based on the sequence of commands a user has executed. The tool generates visual diagrams showing how different Git commands relate to each other. She demonstrates it with Selena Deckelmann's workflow, which clearly illustrates when to use rebase and stash in a typical workflow.
quotes.key_insight: Visualizing the sequence of Git commands you actually use can reveal workflow patterns and clarify when different commands are most useful.

quotes.quote: I really like how this one makes it clear when you'd want to use rebase (in between commit and push), or stash (before checkout and pull). It is a thing of beauty.

quotes.quote: Allison had the amazing idea of subclassing my socket class to log every time I set the state attribute.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recounts a productive day at Hacker School covering three topics: using Python's __setattr__ to automatically test TCP state transitions, learning about floating point number representation, and exploring the idea of visualizing Git workflows from shell history. She worked with Allison Kaptur on a clever testing approach for her TCP client, attended a lecture on floats by Stefan Karpinski, and brainstormed with Philip Guo about using command history files to understand how people actually use tools. The post concludes with her realization that social interaction is her most effective learning channel.
quotes.key_insight: The most effective way to learn technical tools and concepts is through social interaction and collaboration rather than solo documentation reading.

quotes.quote: I tried to work more on making sure they had a good time than making them Learn the Maximum Amount of Things, and I think that went pretty well.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recounts running Python workshops for high school girls at PyData NYC, organized by NumFOCUS with Hacker School volunteers. The beginner group learned programming concepts through Ren'Py story games, while the advanced group explored NYC 311 complaint data using Python. Evans emphasizes prioritizing fun over maximizing learning content, noting the students were genuinely engaged with exploring data from their own neighborhoods. The event also featured demos, talks, and a panel where women in tech shared their diverse paths into programming.
quotes.key_insight: Making programming fun and personally relevant through local data exploration is more effective than trying to maximize the amount of technical content taught.

quotes.quote: In particular, I thought this _send_ack() call at the end was a great idea, because you always want to send an ACK! Except when you don't!
quotes.summary: Evans shares her struggle to implement a readable TCP state machine handler function in Python as part of building a TCP stack. She shows two iterations of the code: the first naively sends an ACK after every packet (which turns out to be wrong), and the second handles flags more explicitly but introduces ordering dependencies. She candidly admits that the messiness of her code reflects her incomplete understanding of TCP itself, and that the combinatorial nature of states and flags makes it hard to verify correctness without extensive testing.
quotes.key_insight: The messiness of code implementing a protocol often directly mirrors incomplete understanding of that protocol, and iterating on the code is itself a way of deepening that understanding.

quotes.quote: My confusion about this function largely reflects my confusion about TCP at this point, I think.

quotes.quote: I realized I can construct basically every packet the same way, except for the flags and the payload.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares progress on her TCP stack implementation, describing a key insight from TCP/IP Illustrated: that every TCP packet except the initial SYN can have the ACK flag set. This realization significantly simplified her packet-sending code. She's taking a correctness-first approach by ACKing every received packet, and has started thinking about implementing bind() for server-side TCP, which turns out to be surprisingly complex. She also celebrates how tests enabled a major refactoring.
quotes.key_insight: A correctness-first approach—doing the simple, technically valid thing before optimizing—is a powerful strategy when building complex protocol implementations from scratch.

quotes.quote: Right now the way I'm handling ACKs is basically to send an ACK for every packet I receive. This isn't really efficient... But it is not incorrect, which is all that I'm going for.

quotes.quote: I realized I'd been having a lot of trouble testing this TCP library yesterday, because all of the tests needed to use the network.
quotes.summary: Julia describes improving her TCP library's test suite by replacing network-dependent tests with mock-based unit tests. The tests went from taking 5-10 seconds to 0.009 seconds and became deterministic. She acknowledges the implementation is still messy and she's stuck on implementing the TCP state machine, so she plans to study a minimal C implementation of TCP for guidance.
quotes.key_insight: Replacing network-dependent tests with mocks makes tests dramatically faster and deterministic, which is essential for making progress on complex networking code.

quotes.quote: We talked about which borough complains the most about rats (the Bronx) and which days of the week have the most noise complaints (Saturday, Sunday).
quotes.summary: Julia Evans gave a talk at NYC Python about using IPython Notebook for data analysis, using NYC's 311 calls dataset as a practical example. The talk explored real urban data questions like which borough complains most about rats (the Bronx) and which days have the most noise complaints (Saturday and Sunday). She shared the notebook for others to try themselves.
quotes.key_insight: Real-world public datasets like NYC 311 calls make data analysis tools like IPython Notebook immediately engaging and accessible to learn.

quotes.quote: Yesterday I realized that I really hadn't been writing as much code as I want to be, though.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans describes starting to build a minimal TCP stack in Python as a way to learn networking through hands-on coding. She's using scapy to handle low-level packet construction while implementing the TCP state machine herself, including socket management and a listener thread. So far she can establish connections and send data, but can't yet parse responses or properly tear down connections. The project is revealing practical challenges like managing half-dead connections and servers treating her implementation as unreliable.
quotes.key_insight: Building a TCP stack from scratch, even an incomplete one, teaches you more about networking than reading about it because the real-world problems you hit — zombie connections, threading issues, server behavior — are the actual substance of how TCP works.

quotes.quote: It turned out that the problem we were having was not at the TCP level, but actually at HTTP level
quotes.summary: Julia describes her progress building a TCP stack from scratch, where she and Jessica managed to successfully receive HTTP responses by sending properly formatted TCP packets to example.com. The main breakthrough was realizing the problem was at the HTTP level (needing proper headers with CRLF line endings), not TCP. She successfully reassembled TCP packets into a webpage but notes several remaining tasks like handling FIN-ACK and deduplicating packets. She reflects on a less productive day due to giving a talk at NYC Python, and expresses eagerness to move toward building something concrete like a bittorrent client.
quotes.key_insight: When debugging networking code, the problem often lives at a different layer of the stack than you expect — here an HTTP formatting issue masqueraded as a TCP problem.

quotes.quote: Today I started trying to write a TCP stack. In Python. We will see if this is a good idea.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her first attempt at writing a TCP stack in Python using scapy. She gets a TCP three-way handshake working but runs into a problem: her own machine's existing network stack sends RST packets in response to unexpected SYN-ACK replies. She solves this by using a fake IP address and ARP broadcasting to bypass her OS's interference. The post captures the messy, iterative reality of learning networking by building things from scratch.
quotes.key_insight: Building a TCP stack from userspace forces you to confront the reality that your OS already has one, and it will fight you for control of the network unless you find creative workarounds.

quotes.quote: I already have a network stack on my machine, and it was like "what's this SYN-ACK packet? I didn't ask for this!". So it would just reset the connection.

quotes.quote: IP packets have a ttl attribute, which stands for "Time-To-Live". Every time a machine receives an IP packet, it decreases the ttl by 1 and passes it on. Basically this is a super smart way to make sure that packets don't get into infinite loops.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans implements traceroute in just 16 lines of Python using the Scapy library. She explains how IP packets have a TTL (Time-To-Live) attribute that decrements at each hop, and how traceroute exploits this by sending packets with incrementally increasing TTLs. When a packet's TTL expires, the intermediate router sends back an ICMP error, revealing its address. The implementation sends UDP packets with increasing TTLs and checks whether the reply indicates a timeout (keep going) or a destination reached (done).
quotes.key_insight: Traceroute is a surprisingly simple concept — just send packets with incrementing TTLs and read the ICMP errors back — and can be implemented in about 15 lines of Python with the right library.

quotes.quote: If a packet's ttl runs out before it replies, the last machine sends back an ICMP packet saying "sorry, failed!".

quotes.quote: For a long time I've had the notion that networking is pretty complicated, but I didn't really know why that was.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her experience trying to learn networking by attempting a specific exploit (replacing webpages with ponies on her phone). She discovers several concrete reasons why networking is fundamentally complicated: protocols evolve but implementations lag behind, and the abstraction layers that are supposed to simplify things end up leaking. The post uses iptables and TCP delayed ACK as specific examples of these broader problems.
quotes.key_insight: Networking is hard not just because of its complexity, but because its abstraction layers leak — higher-level code that shouldn't need to care about lower-level protocols often does.

quotes.quote: What this line basically does is "Hey phone! You want to send a packet to the router? Send that to me instead. Thanks!"
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her experiment with ARP cache poisoning, a technique for intercepting network packets on a wireless network. She explains how ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) works by translating IP addresses to MAC addresses, and how the protocol's trust-based design makes it trivially exploitable. Using the arpspoof tool, she demonstrates how you can trick a device into sending its packets to your machine instead of the router. She notes some devices are more susceptible than others, with her Linux machine resisting the attack while a Macbook Pro was vulnerable.
quotes.key_insight: ARP's fundamental design flaw is that devices trust unsolicited MAC address updates from anyone on the network, making packet interception trivially easy with a single command.

quotes.quote: It's not just that anyone can reply, they can reply even if the phone didn't ask for a MAC address.

quotes.quote: Oh look! It turns out that gcc already knows about this exploit and has special built-in protections! We can disable those, though…
quotes.summary: Evans walks through creating a basic buffer overflow exploit as part of her Hacker School security week, inspired by Jon Erickson's 'Hacking: The Art of Exploitation.' She demonstrates a vulnerable C program that uses gets() to read into a fixed-size buffer, then shows how to overwrite the return address to redirect execution to an arbitrary function. The post covers practical details like disabling gcc's stack protector, dealing with little-endian byte ordering, and the difference between 32-bit and 64-bit exploitation. She notes the existence of more sophisticated techniques like NOP sleds but sticks with the simpler brute-force approach.
quotes.key_insight: Buffer overflows exploit the fact that functions like gets() write past buffer boundaries, allowing an attacker to overwrite the return address on the stack and redirect program execution.

quotes.quote: I got curious about which unix command line utilities were the most popular, so I ran a survey on Hacker News.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares a visualization she built from a Hacker News survey of 1,500 responses about Unix command line utility usage. She was curious about which commands are most popular and how they correlate with each other. The result is an interactive graph showing which commands are strongly correlated in usage, such as ruby and rails. She highlights the sparse area around gcc as particularly interesting.
quotes.key_insight: Surveying real-world command usage patterns and visualizing their correlations reveals distinct ecosystem clusters among Unix users.

quotes.quote: Gzip compresses by replacing text with pointers to earlier parts of the text.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans created a visualization of gzip decompression applied to Edgar Allan Poe's 'The Raven,' showing how the LZ77 algorithm replaces text with pointers to earlier parts of the document. The visualization highlights copied text segments during decompression, revealing how poetic structures like rhyming naturally emerge in the compression patterns. She built this by implementing gunzip from scratch and adding print/sleep statements to show the process. The post includes links to visualizations of the full poem and Hamlet.
quotes.key_insight: Visualizing gzip decompression on poetry reveals how compression algorithms naturally detect and exploit the repeated structures that make poems poetic, like rhyme and refrain.

quotes.quote: The basic idea behind gzip (aka the DEFLATE algorithm) is that there are 2 passes.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents the internal structure of gzip files after implementing gunzip in Julia. She walks through the DEFLATE algorithm's two passes—LZ77 for replacing repeated sequences with pointers, and Huffman coding for efficient representation. The post details the file format layer by layer: headers, metadata, block structure, the recursive Huffman tree encoding scheme, and the actual decompression logic. She shares her Julia data structures and code, noting the surprising complexity of details like off-by-one constants and the non-obvious split between literal and distance trees.
quotes.key_insight: The gzip file format's apparent simplicity conceals recursive complexity—Huffman trees encoded with other Huffman trees, off-by-one constants, and interdependent blocks—that only becomes visible when you implement a decompressor from scratch.

quotes.quote: There is a recursive thing going on where there is a Huffman tree encoded with another Huffman tree.

quotes.quote: When you're not sure exactly what your code is supposed to be doing or how to verify whether it's correct is exactly when it's the most helpful to write unit tests.
quotes.summary: Evans reflects on struggling with an off-by-one error in her gzip implementation and realizing that the situations where testing feels hardest are exactly when tests are most valuable. After repeatedly telling people she couldn't write tests because she didn't know what intermediate results should look like, a colleague suggested using a reference C implementation to generate expected test data. She acknowledges that writing more tests would help her identify which parts of her code she's confident about versus which parts likely contain bugs.
quotes.key_insight: The moments when writing tests feels hardest—when you don't fully understand your code's expected behavior—are precisely when tests provide the most value.

quotes.quote: Today I spent most of the day figuring out that n_to_read = head.hlit + head.hdist + 257 should be n_to_read = head.hlit + head.hdist + 258
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recounts spending most of a day debugging a single off-by-one error in her gzip decompression implementation. The fix was changing 257 to 258 in a calculation, though she still doesn't fully understand why. Despite the frustration, she's nearly able to decompress gzipped files. The post captures the unglamorous reality of low-level programming work.
quotes.key_insight: Sometimes debugging means spending an entire day finding a single off-by-one error, and that's just part of the process.

quotes.quote: And I still don't know why, exactly.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recounts spending most of a day debugging a single off-by-one error in her gzip decompression implementation. The fix was changing 257 to 258 in a calculation, though she still doesn't fully understand why. Despite the frustration, she's nearly able to decompress gzipped files. The post captures the unglamorous reality of low-level programming work.
quotes.key_insight: Sometimes debugging means spending an entire day finding a single off-by-one error, and that's just part of the process.

quotes.quote: I will never get tired of the fact that Julia is called Julia.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her impressions of the Julia programming language after a week of use during Hacker School. She highlights its appealing combination of Python-like ease and C-like performance, its IPython notebook integration, and the ability to inspect LLVM code for functions. She notes that much of Julia is written in Julia itself, making it accessible for contributors. Her complaints are minor, mostly around REPL startup time and session instability when redefining functions.
quotes.key_insight: Julia's power lies in combining the ease of a dynamic scripting language with the performance transparency of a systems language, all while being largely self-hosted.

quotes.quote: Spoiler: I don't really know yet, but there is a lot of mucking with bits.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her early exploration of how gzip compression works, undertaken as a project at Hacker School. She's writing a parallel version of gzip in the Julia programming language. The post covers the basic building blocks of gzip (Huffman coding, LZ77 compression) and notes that the real complexity isn't in the algorithms themselves but in the bit-level fiddling required for efficiency. She shares a small Julia code snippet for converting bits to bytes and admits she's only just started decoding the gzip header.
quotes.key_insight: The conceptual algorithms behind gzip (Huffman coding, LZ77) aren't the hard part — the real complexity lives in the bit-level fiddling needed to make the encoding efficient.

quotes.quote: These things *sound* like they would be the complicated part, but so far they don't seem to be too bad. Conceptually. I think.

quotes.quote: Still hunting for something which is the right size and which I can care about enough to do it.
quotes.summary: Julia reflects on her goals midway through Hacker School, realizing that while she's contributing to open source, she hasn't made progress on writing non-trivial software from scratch or improving her code design skills. She identifies five goals including learning concurrency and functional programming, and considers that reading more well-written code might help. She floats the idea of writing a JavaScript interpreter as a project that could address multiple goals at once.
quotes.key_insight: Improving at code design may require spending more time reading well-written code, not just writing more code.

quotes.quote: The CPython interpreter is mostly in one 3,500 file called ceval.c. The main part of this file is a 2,000-line switch statement – switch(opcode) {.... Ack.
quotes.summary: Evans documents her experience pairing with Hacker School facilitator Allison on a Python bytecode interpreter, discovering that CPython's internals are surprisingly approachable. She explores ceval.c, the 3,500-line file containing CPython's main interpreter loop, and finds that individual opcodes like BINARY_SUBTRACT are straightforward to understand. The post walks through what a single opcode does step by step, demystifying the C code behind Python execution. Her core realization is that bytecode is literally made of bytes—fewer than 256 instructions, each represented by a single byte.
quotes.key_insight: The C code that implements Python's interpreter is far more approachable than it sounds—individual bytecode operations are simple, readable functions, and demystifying them just requires looking at the source.

quotes.quote: The thing I forgot about open source is that open source (for me!) is *scary* and *hard*.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her excitement about contributing to the Julia programming language during Hacker School's maintainers program, where open source maintainers help students contribute to projects. She lists several reasons Julia appeals to her, including that much of Julia is written in Julia itself and its focus on scientific computing. She then honestly confronts the emotional difficulty of open source contribution—the fear of public discussion, the frustration of spending hours on tiny bugs, and the vulnerability of being visibly new. She resolves that facing this discomfort is exactly why she's at Hacker School and celebrates making pull requests that day.
quotes.key_insight: Open source contribution is genuinely scary and emotionally hard, but deliberately leaning into that discomfort—especially with support from others—is how you get past the fear.

quotes.quote: This is really just procedural code written in Clojure. I can't tell yet if this is an appropriate way to write a small Clojure program.
quotes.summary: Julia documents her seventh day at Hacker School where she built a simple UDP echo server in Clojure. While also working on a kernel module that isn't ready yet, she shares the echo server as a quick win. She notes the code is essentially procedural code written in Clojure and isn't sure if that's idiomatic. The project is a first step toward potentially writing a BitTorrent client in Clojure, inspired by other Hacker School participants.
quotes.key_insight: Small, working projects serve as both learning exercises and stepping stones toward more ambitious goals.

quotes.quote: I made some small improvements on my kernel module from yesterday – I made it into a rootkit!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents writing a simple rootkit as a kernel module during her Hacker School experience. The rootkit allows any unprivileged user to become root by writing a PID to /proc/buddyinfo. She explains how it works by hijacking file_operations structs to add a write handler to a read-only proc file, which then changes process ownership to root. She credits the code to an existing rootkit she studied and simplified.
quotes.key_insight: Building a rootkit is a surprisingly effective way to learn how Linux kernel modules and file operations work, because it demonstrates the kernel's unrestricted power over process ownership and file handlers.

quotes.quote: What I mean by a "rootkit" is a kernel module that once I put it in my kernel, any unprivileged user who knows the right incantation can become root.

quotes.quote: I WROTE A KERNEL MODULE. It doesn't do anything useful or anything, but still!
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares her excitement about writing her first Linux kernel module during Hacker School. The module uses the Netfilter framework to intercept incoming packets and print 'Hello packet' to the kernel log. While it doesn't do anything useful, the exercise taught her fundamental differences between kernel-space and user-space programming, like the inability to use glibc functions. She shares resources for others interested in kernel module development.
quotes.key_insight: Kernel module development is more accessible than it seems, and even a simple packet-intercepting module reveals fundamental differences between kernel-space and user-space programming.

quotes.quote: You can't use malloc inside the kernel (?!!?). This is because anything that's used in the kernel needs to be defined in the kernel, and malloc is in glibc.

quotes.quote: People sometimes ask me where the feed is for this data, as it's not always advertised on their website.
quotes.summary: This short reference post collects the data feed URLs for bike sharing systems across North America, London, and Melbourne. The feeds, provided by Bixi-powered systems and others, publish real-time station availability data in XML or JSON format, updated roughly every minute. Evans notes that these feeds aren't always easy to find on official websites, motivating her to compile them in one place. She also mentions occasional irregularities in feed formats.
quotes.key_insight: Publicly available real-time data feeds for bike sharing systems exist but are often undocumented, making a curated reference list valuable for developers who want to hack on transit data.

quotes.quote: Processes each have their own PID and address space, threads share the PID and address space of a single process.
quotes.summary: Evans recounts a Linux Kernel Club session at Hacker School where she learned about the difference between processes and threads. The post explains that processes have their own PID and address space while threads share both. She breaks down what address space includes (code, data, heap, stack, environment variables) and lists the tradeoffs of threads: easier communication but risk of race conditions. The group also discussed kernel modules as a good entry point for kernel development.
quotes.key_insight: The fundamental distinction between processes and threads comes down to address space isolation: processes get their own memory for safety, while threads share memory for efficiency at the cost of concurrency complexity.

quotes.quote: We had a really fun session this morning where we got together and made a list of the different functions that Linux kernel takes care of.
quotes.summary: Evans documents a collaborative session at Hacker School where participants brainstormed the different functions handled by the Linux kernel. The post presents a comprehensive list covering memory management, device drivers, process scheduling, filesystems, networking, and more. It serves as a beginner-friendly inventory of kernel responsibilities, written during her early days of kernel exploration. The post reflects her learning-in-public approach, inviting corrections from readers.
quotes.key_insight: The Linux kernel's responsibilities span an enormous range of functions from hardware management to process scheduling to networking, and mapping them all out is a useful first step toward understanding how operating systems work.

quotes.quote: Before yesterday, I didn't have a very clear idea of what writing a shell even *meant*.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her early exploration of writing a shell in C during Hacker School, breaking down what a shell actually needs to do. She lists the core responsibilities: parsing commands, glob expansion, pipes and redirection, signal handling, process management, and shell scripting. She also notes what the shell doesn't handle — PATH resolution is done by exec. The post captures the moment of demystifying something fundamental by trying to build it from scratch.
quotes.key_insight: You don't truly understand a tool until you try to build it yourself, and listing what it must do is the first step toward that understanding.
