quotes.quote: So this looks like normal code that would print 'banana' twice.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recounts her first day at Hacker School, where she paired with another student to build a shell in C. They encountered a subtle bug where a local array allocated on the stack was accessed after the function returned, causing corrupted output. The post walks through a minimal code example showing how a pointer to stack-allocated data becomes invalid once the function scope ends. Evans expresses excitement about encountering low-level bugs that would be impossible in higher-level languages like Python.
quotes.key_insight: Returning a pointer to stack-allocated data in C creates a dangling pointer that silently corrupts when the stack is reused, a class of bug that simply cannot exist in memory-managed languages.

quotes.quote: I spent an hour or two trying to understand how to autotools and it made me want to kill someone.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans documents her pre-Hacker School preparation, attempting to implement Snake in C using ncurses as a way to improve her low-level programming skills. The Snake implementation went smoothly, but unit testing in C proved to be the real challenge. After struggling with autotools for hours, she discovered checkmk, a simpler awk-based tool that made writing tests for the check framework painless. She concludes that Snake was too easy and considers writing a shell next.
quotes.key_insight: The tooling and infrastructure around C development (build systems, testing frameworks) can be a bigger obstacle than the actual programming, but simpler alternatives exist if you look for them.

quotes.quote: I was really happy and surprised not too long ago when I realized that everything I do is a 'thing women do' (and a 'thing programmers do'), because I do them.
quotes.summary: Evans reviews Julia Serano's book 'Excluded: Making Feminist and Queer Movements More Inclusive,' calling it one of the best purchases she's made. She highlights Serano's argument against the myth that all women share a common bond, noting that placing people in boxes suffocates them. Evans connects this to her own realization that everything she does is inherently 'a thing women do' and 'a thing programmers do.' The post touches on allyship, inclusivity, and how communities sometimes exclude people by demanding they be radical enough.
quotes.key_insight: Labels and identities should be tools for finding common ground, not boxes that constrain who gets to belong.

quotes.quote: In my mind, there's no reason to write C++ anymore
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recaps PyData Boston 2013, highlighting talks she enjoyed including Travis Oliphant's keynote about Continuum Analytics, Lynn Cherny's talk on detecting sex scenes in Fifty Shades of Grey using machine learning, and Jeff Bezanson's introduction to the Julia programming language. She shares enthusiasm about the people she met and the tools she discovered. The post is a brief, energetic conference recap focused on the people and ideas that excited her most.
quotes.key_insight: Conference value comes as much from discovering inspiring people and communities as from the technical content of talks.

quotes.quote: Open data apps are only useful if they're solving problems that people are really having, and answering questions that people really need to know the answers to.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans recaps the Open Data Exchange 2013 conference in Montreal, highlighting talks and panels about making open data useful to real people. A recurring theme was the importance of humanizing data and connecting technologists with citizens, business owners, and domain experts. She highlights lightning talks covering topics from historical aerial photo overlays to journalist-programmer collaboration, and panels discussing how open data has saved Canada billions and how in-person engagement beats online outreach. The post reflects her enthusiasm for the community and the practical, people-centered approach to open data advocacy.
quotes.key_insight: Open data work is most impactful when technologists prioritize connecting with real people and their actual problems over building technically interesting projects.

quotes.quote: I showed some graphs of # bikes vs temperature and # bikes vs raininess, using data downloaded from the Canadian National Climate Archive.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans shares a talk she gave at Montreal Python about graphing bike path usage data from Montreal's open data portal. She combined bike counter sensor data with weather data from Canada's National Climate Archive to explore relationships between cycling and weather conditions. The analysis was done using pandas and IPython Notebook, and she links to the viewable notebook. She invites readers to download the notebook and do their own analysis at an upcoming Python Night event.
quotes.key_insight: Combining publicly available open datasets with tools like pandas and IPython Notebook makes exploratory data analysis accessible and shareable.

quotes.quote: Decided that I was tired of messing with HTML and CSS and layouts to set up Jekyll, so trying out Octopress here instead.
quotes.summary: Julia Evans briefly documents her decision to switch from Jekyll to Octopress for her blog. She found Jekyll's HTML, CSS, and layout configuration tedious, and Octopress offered an easier setup experience. The post is essentially a first impression noting that things look okay so far. Her main remaining concern is customizing the color scheme to be more orange.
quotes.key_insight: Sometimes the best tool is the one that gets out of your way and lets you focus on what you actually care about.
